words.definition: Become less intense or widespread.
words.example: After an hour in the cellar, we breathed easier as the sound of the wind began to abate.
words.origin: Middle English: from Old French abatre 'to fell', from a- (from Latin ad 'to, at') + batre 'to beat' (from Latin battere).

words.definition: A beginner, neophyte.
words.example: I wanted to join the school chess team, but soon realized I was an abecedarian when compared to the current members.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from late Latin abecedarius 'alphabetical', based on the names of the letters a, b, c, d.

words.definition: Renounce or reject (something desired or valuable).
words.example: He abnegated the family inheritance to make a name for himself on his own.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin abnegat- 'renounced', from the verb abnegare, from ab- 'away' + negare 'deny'.

words.definition: Loathsome, odious or detestable.
words.example: Jennifer's neglect of her pets was abominable and inexcusable.
words.origin: Middle English: via Old French from Latin abominabilis, from abominari 'deprecate'.

words.definition: To rub or wear away, especially by friction.
words.example: He paced his office relentlessly when making a decision, abrading the carpet with the soles of his shoes over the course of a decade.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin abradere, from ab- 'off, away' + radere 'to scrape'.

words.definition: Leave hurriedly and secretly, typically to avoid detection of or arrest for an unlawful action such as theft.
words.example: The burglar absconded with the money before anyone noticed he'd entered the house.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin abscondere 'hide', from ab- 'away, from' + condere 'stow'.

words.definition: To discreetly leave a gathering or party without informing the host.
words.example: At the party, I made such a fool of myself that I felt it was best to absquatulate after a half hour.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: a humorous pseudo-Latin formation in American English, suggesting 'to make off'.

words.definition: Not self-indulgent, especially when eating and drinking.
words.example: He was very abstemious when with a group, limiting himself to one beer and only a handful of chips.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin abstemius (from ab- 'from' + a word related to temetum 'alcoholic liquor').

words.definition: Difficult to understand; obscure.
words.example: He looked through the ancient book, trying to make sense of its abstruse wording and concepts.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Latin abstrusus 'put away, hidden', from abstrudere 'conceal', from ab- 'from' + trudere 'to thrust'.

words.definition: Spiritual or mental sloth; apathy.
words.example: When she broke up with him, he fell into a state of acedia and didn't leave the apartment for two months.
words.origin: Late 19th century: from late Latin, from Greek akēdia 'listlessness', from a- 'without' + kēdos 'care'.

words.definition: Make unable to think clearly; confuse.
words.example: Being in love addled the young man, causing him to rethink every belief he'd ever held on the subject.
words.origin: Old English adela 'liquid filth', later 'rotten egg', giving rise to the sense 'muddle, confuse'.

words.definition: Clever or skillful in using the hands or mind.
words.example: Our financial planner was adroit at steering us around tax laws and finding loopholes as we started our business.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from French, from à droit 'according to right, properly'.

words.definition: The protection, backing or support of a particular person or organization.
words.example: The negotiations were conduced under the aegis of the United Nations.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin, from Greek aigis 'shield of Zeus'.

words.definition: A person who is pretentious, windy or full of hot air.
words.example: He droned on for 40 minutes, an aeolist who loved to hear himself vomit his empty words on a glassy-eyed crowd.
words.origin: Late 17th century: coined by Jonathan Swift from Latin Aeolus, god of the winds, + -ist.

words.definition: An instance of fighting in a public place that disturbs the peace.
words.example: It began in the bar, but the affray soon took the brawlers into the crowded streets.
words.origin: Middle English: from Old French esfrei, from esfreer 'disturb', based on Latin ex- 'out' + Frankish frithu 'peace'.

words.definition: One who never laughs.
words.example: He was a sour old man, an agelast who hadn't cracked a smile in decades.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from Greek agelastos 'not laughing', from a- 'not' + gelan 'to laugh'.

words.definition: Frightened or alarmed.
words.example: Despite his boasts, Fred spent the entire film aglifft, at one point tossing his popcorn into the air in alarm.
words.origin: Scots and Northern English dialect: probably from a- + a variant related to 'gliff' meaning a sudden fright.

words.definition: Full of intense interest or excitement.
words.example: Timmy was agog as he stepped into the theme park and saw all his favorite cartoon characters walking around, shaking hands.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: probably from Old French en gogues 'in merriment', from gogue 'fun'.

words.definition: Brisk and cheerful readiness.
words.example: The young intern burst through the door, brimming with alacrity.
words.origin: Late 15th century: from Latin alacritas, from alacer 'brisk, lively'.

words.definition: To leave a conveyance such as a bus or taxi.
words.example: I will alight at the next stop.
words.origin: Old English ālīhtan, from ā- (intensive prefix) + līhtan 'descend, make light'.

words.definition: Walk or move at a slow, relaxed pace.
words.example: With nowhere to be, he spent the afternoon ambling down the quaint streets of his hometown.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French ambler, from Latin ambulare 'to walk'.

words.definition: Loss of feelings for someone who was formerly loved.
words.example: They sat in silence in their usual coffee shop, the anagapesis growing as their drinks cooled.
words.origin: Modern coinage: from Greek ana- 'back, again' + agapesis 'affection, love'.

words.definition: Something or someone that one vehemently dislikes.
words.example: Supporting such a vile, bigoted candidate was anathema to the young voter.
words.origin: Early 16th century: from ecclesiastical Latin, 'excommunicated person, curse', from Greek anathema 'thing dedicated', later 'thing devoted to evil'.

words.definition: A conversation in which everyone is talking, but no one is listening.
words.example: What started as a civil debate turned into a deafening anecdoche, in which every person believed they possessed the ideal solution.
words.origin: Modern coinage: a blend of anecdote and synecdoche, coined in The Dictionary of Obscure Sorrows.

words.definition: Nostalgia for a place or time one has never known or experienced.
words.example: The photo of a bygone era filled her with a strange feeling of anemoia.
words.origin: Modern coinage: from Greek anemos 'wind' + noos 'mind', coined in The Dictionary of Obscure Sorrows.

words.definition: Indirect and containing bends, turns or winds; circuitous.
words.example: The road used to reach the castle was anfractuous.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from late Latin anfractuosus, from anfractus 'a bending', from an- + frangere 'to break'.

words.definition: Inability to feel pleasure.
words.example: Despite the fact that he was sitting on the beach in the middle of summer, a crippling anhedonia overtook John, making him yearn for a quiet bedroom to pass the day in alone.
words.origin: Late 19th century: French anhédonie, from Greek an- 'without' + hēdonē 'pleasure'.

words.definition: Criticism or censure.
words.example: Every remark she made was an animadversion over the way he'd done his job.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin animadversio(n-), from animadvertere 'turn the mind to', from animus 'mind' + advertere 'turn to'.

words.definition: Not likely to provoke dissent or offense; inoffensive, often deliberately so.
words.example: Tim rolled his eyes as his wife read the latest anodyne essay from "Chicken Soup for the Soul."
words.origin: Mid 16th century (as a noun denoting a painkiller): via Latin from Greek anōdunos 'painless', from an- 'without' + odunē 'pain'.

words.definition: Very old, old-fashioned, out of date, antiquated, primitive. Literally "before the flood," referring by implication to the Biblical tale of Noah.
words.example: "This company's vacation policy is positively antediluvian, so I'm giving you four weeks off during the coming year."
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin ante 'before' + diluvium 'deluge' + -an.

words.definition: Third from the end. The one before the next-to-the-last.
words.example: December 29 is the antepenultimate day of the year.
words.origin: Mid 18th century: from Latin ante 'before' + paenultimus 'last but one', from paene 'almost' + ultimus 'last'.

words.definition: Coming before in time; earlier.
words.example: The fossil was from an era anterior to the appearance of man.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin, comparative of ante 'before'.

words.definition: Opposite.
words.example: Despite a cultural obsession with acquisition, objective poverty and happiness are not antithetical.
words.origin: Early 17th century: via late Latin from Greek antithetikos, from antithesis 'placing against'.

words.definition: A pithy observation that contains a general truth.
words.example: Grandpa always had an aphorism at the ready to help me navigate life's dilemmas.
words.origin: Early 16th century: from French aphorisme or late Latin aphorismus, from Greek aphorismos 'definition', from aphorizein 'define'.

words.definition: Feeling intense rage or fury to the point of virtual paralysis; relating to a stroke (apoplexy).
words.example: Emma became apoplectic when she saw her tipsy husband flirting with a hussy in the bar.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from French apoplectique, via late Latin from Greek apoplēktikos, from apoplēssein 'disable by a stroke'.

words.definition: A person who renounces a religious or political belief or principle.
words.example: The day his church outlawed drinking was the day Jim became an apostate.
words.origin: Middle English: from ecclesiastical Latin apostata, from Greek apostatēs 'runaway slave', from aphistanai 'stand away'.

words.definition: A person who prepared and sold medicines and drugs.
words.example: His child's cough echoing into the night, the settler rode off on his steed to summon the local apothecary.
words.origin: Late Middle English: via Old French from late Latin apothecarius, from Greek apothēkē 'storehouse'.

words.definition: The highest point in the development of something; culmination or climax.
words.example: His keynote speech was the apotheosis of his career.
words.origin: Late 16th century: via ecclesiastical Latin from Greek apotheōsis, from apotheoun 'make a god of', from apo- + theos 'god'.

words.definition: A fixed and strong desire.
words.example: The film had such strong product placement that John found himself with an inexplicable appetency for fast food, which he normally avoided.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin appetentia, from appetere 'seek after', from ad- 'to' + petere 'seek'.

words.definition: Approval, sanction or commendation.
words.example: Most of us desire the approbation of others, though it may be hard to admit.
words.origin: Late Middle English: via Old French from Latin approbatio(n-), from approbare 'approve'.

words.definition: To sunbathe or bask in the sun.
words.example: He took advantage of the summer day, apricating by the pool for most of the afternoon.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin apricari 'to bask in the sun', from apricus 'exposed to the sun'.

words.definition: Pastoral, rural, in a peaceful natural setting.
words.example: When Leeman retired he left the city and built a tiny house in a quiet, arcadian corner of the Berkshires.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Latin Arcadia, a mountainous region of Greece idealized as a place of rustic simplicity.

words.definition: Understood by few; mysterious or secret.
words.example: Semiotics is an arcane theory often touted as the science of imagery.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin arcanus, from arcere 'to shut up', from arca 'chest'.

words.definition: Resources available for a certain purpose.
words.example: Her dressing table was filled with colognes and makeup of all sorts -- the standard armamentarium of seduction.
words.origin: Late 19th century: from Latin armamentarium 'arsenal', from armamenta 'implements, equipment'.

words.definition: An ambitious or ruthlessly self-seeking person, especially one who has recently acquired wealth or social status.
words.example: The young arriviste combed the newspaper looking for stories lauding his success and trumpeting his enemies' failures.
words.origin: Early 20th century: from French, from arriver 'to arrive', literally 'one who has arrived'.

words.definition: Take or claim (something) without justification.
words.example: Within five minutes of meeting the members of his project team, he arrogated leadership and began barking orders.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin arrogat- 'claimed for oneself', from the verb arrogare, from ad- 'to' + rogare 'ask'.

words.definition: To make something less painful or severe; to lessen the intensity of; to mitigate.
words.example: I pray that our Heavenly Father may assuage the anguish of your bereavement, and leave you only the cherished memory of the loved and lost... (Abraham Lincoln, letter to Mrs. Bixby, Nov. 1864)
words.origin: Middle English: from Old French assouagier, from Latin ad- 'to' + suavis 'sweet'.

words.definition: Deathlessness; immortality.
words.example: The potion granted athanasia to whoever drank it.
words.origin: Early 18th century: from Greek athanasia 'immortality', from a- 'without' + thanatos 'death'.

words.definition: Respected and impressive.
words.example: He took his seat among the symphony members, proud to be part of such an august institution.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin augustus 'consecrated, venerable'.

words.definition: Go away.
words.example: "Avaunt, ye specter," he cried out in the night, hoping to chase the shadows from his room.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French avant 'forward', from late Latin abante, from ab 'from' + ante 'before'.

words.definition: Of or relating to an uncle, or resembling an uncle in attitude; kindly, genial, benevolent.
words.example: Despite Paolo's avuncular demeanor, Alessandra began to suspect that his motives were rather less than honorable.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from Latin avunculus 'maternal uncle', diminutive of avus 'grandfather'.

words.definition: Relating to drunkenness and mad revelry. From Bacchus, the Greek god of wine.
words.example: Upset with the tourists' bacchanalian behavior, the bartender called the police.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Latin Bacchanalia, the Roman festival of Bacchus, the god of wine.

words.definition: Area of interest or skill.
words.example: "Star Wars" was Jack's bailiwick, and he could go on for hours about characters who had appeared on screen for less than 30 seconds.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from bailie 'bailiff' + wick, from Old English wīc 'dwelling place'.

words.definition: Nonsense.
words.example: I know balderdash; after all, I've heard ... um, who was that governor of Alaska?
words.origin: Late 16th century (denoting a froth or jumbled mixture of liquids): of unknown origin.

words.definition: Threatening harm; menacing.
words.example: The dog sat on the porch, greeting all pedestrians with a baleful snarl.
words.origin: Old English bealufull, from bealu 'evil' (see bale) + -ful.

words.definition: Extravagant publicity or fuss
words.example: Despite all of the ballyhoo in the papers and on television, audiences stayed away and the play closed three weeks later.
words.origin: Late 19th century (originally US): of unknown origin.

words.definition: To cheat or steal.
words.example: Stop trying to bamboozle me out of my money!
words.origin: Early 18th century: of unknown origin; perhaps from Scots bombaze 'perplex' or French embabouiner 'to make a fool of'.

words.definition: Producing an unintentional effect of anticlimax.
words.example: The opening bars of the recital were glorious, but introducing a kazoo solo was a bathetic effort.
words.origin: Late 18th century: from bathos, on the pattern of pathos and pathetic.

words.definition: To smile broadly and radiantly. A versatile word, too infrequently used this way.
words.example: Amy was beaming as she came down the aisle, but to her mom her expression seemed less like a smile of joy than a rictus of terror.
words.origin: Old English bēam 'tree, beam of light', of Germanic origin; the verb sense 'smile radiantly' developed in the 19th century.

words.definition: A noisy, festive party or celebration.
words.example: Each year the Guild of Freemen met for a great beano in the Guildhall, a banquet of many courses; at the culmination of the evening the Archbishop of Canterbury made a speech.(Eric Newby, Something Wholesale, 1962)
words.origin: Late 19th century: probably an abbreviation of beanfeast, a celebratory dinner once given by employers to employees.

words.definition: Verb. 1. To confuse, perplex or bewilder. 2. To stupefy as if with alcoholic drinks.
words.example: "She couldn't say whether she had been more befuddled by his constant prattling -- or the three jumbo martinis."
words.origin: Late 19th century: from be- (as an intensifier) + the dialect word fuddle 'to confuse'.

words.definition: Tear-stained.
words.example: He finished writing the letter, stuffed the begrutten, ink-smudged paper into an envelope and mailed it to the woman he used to love.
words.origin: Scots dialect: past participle of greet 'to weep', from Old English grētan 'to weep'.

words.definition: A beautiful and good woman.
words.example: My grandmother was known as a the town bellibone, always with a smile on her face and goodness in her heart.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from French belle et bonne 'fair and good'.

words.definition: Warlike, aggressive, hostile. President Obama did his part for rescuing this word from obscurity by using it in a recent press conference.
words.example: For pete's sake, spare us the bellicose rhetoric. Just fire your Kalashnikovs into the air for awhile and be done with it.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin bellicosus, from bellicus 'warlike', from bellum 'war'.

words.definition: Deprived; lacking something needed, wanted or expected.
words.example: When her last chihuahua finally died, Aunt Sophie felt bereft of all warmth and selfless companionship.
words.origin: Old English bereafod, past participle of bereave, from be- + reave 'to plunder'.

words.definition: Stand astride over; span or straddle.
words.example: He bestrode the gap between the river, lifting the child over so his shoes wouldn't get wet.
words.origin: Old English bestrīdan, from be- + strīdan 'to stride'.

words.definition: The person to whom one is engaged.
words.example: The father and mother came into the room and gave the betrothed couple their blessing.
words.origin: Middle English: from be- + obsolete trouth 'truth, troth'.

words.definition: Excessively fond of drinking alcohol; referring to the consumption of alcoholic beverages.
words.example: On St. Patrick's Day evening, bibulous celebrants lay crumpled on the streets of Savannah like victims of urban warfare.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin bibulus 'freely drinking', from bibere 'to drink'.

words.definition: Divide into two branches.
words.example: The river bifurcates at the peninsula. (v.)
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from medieval Latin bifurcatus, from Latin bi- 'two' + furca 'a fork'.

words.definition: A novel dealing with one person's formative years or spiritual education.
words.example: The bildungsroman covers the hero's high school journey as he discovers who he is and finds the courage to face life's challenges.
words.origin: Early 20th century: German, from Bildung 'education' + Roman 'a novel'.

words.definition: Spiteful; bad-tempered.
words.example: He was in a bilious mood, given that it was Monday morning and he hadn't yet had his coffee.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from French bilieux, from Latin bilis 'bile'.

words.definition: A person who talks at great length without making much sense.
words.example: The cable TV commentator was a blatherskite who produced 30 minutes of angry nonsense each night.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from blather + skite (a contemptuous term), from Scots.

words.definition: A senseless babbler or boaster.
words.example: The bartender groaned at the string of nonsense coming from the blatteroon in the corner; he could cut off his drinks, but the man was stone-cold sober.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Italian blatterone, from Latin blaterare 'to babble'.

words.definition: Showing a casual and cheerful indifference considered to be callous or improper.
words.example: His blithe attitude toward the police officer did not help him escape a ticket.
words.origin: Old English blīthe 'gentle, kind', of Germanic origin.

words.definition: Senselessly talkative, babbling; used chiefly as an intensive to express annoyance or contempt.
words.example: His Facebook posts were the confused ramblings of a blithering fool.
words.origin: Late 19th century: present participle of blither, variant of blather.

words.definition: To speak at length in a pompous or boastful manner.
words.example: I was totally put off by the winning coaches' tendency to bloviate ad nauseam.
words.origin: Mid 19th century (US): probably from blow 'to boast' with a mock-Latin ending.

words.definition: Unsteady, wobbly, rickety or crooked.
words.example: That table has a bockety leg.
words.origin: 20th century: Hiberno-English, of uncertain origin.

words.definition: Ghost, spirit, bugaboo.
words.example: The bodach appeared in the corner of the room, staring at him as he tried to drift to sleep.
words.origin: From Scottish Gaelic and Irish bodach 'old man, churl, spectre'.

words.definition: Noisy, energetic, and cheerful; rowdy.
words.example: The kindergarten class was particularly boisterous on this, the last Friday of the school year.
words.origin: Late Middle English: variant of earlier boistous 'rustic, coarse', of unknown origin.

words.definition: Buzz; hum.
words.example: A fly bombinated in the corner of the sun porch, making it hard for Tom to relax.
words.origin: Early 20th century: from medieval Latin bombinare, alteration of Latin bombilare 'to buzz'.

words.definition: Cheerful friendliness; geniality.
words.example: He brimmed with bonhomie, enlivening a room as soon as he entered.
words.origin: Late 18th century: from French bonhomie, from bonhomme 'good fellow'.

words.definition: Work or activity that is wasteful or pointless but gives the appearance of having value.
words.example: When you compare the money the government spent on the project to actual results obtained, it becomes clear that this was a massive boondoggle.
words.origin: 1930s (US): of unknown origin, popularized as a term for make-work projects.

words.definition: Woodsy; abundant in bushes, shrubs or trees.
words.example: Edwin's transition to downtown Des Moines from his home in the bosky dells of rural New Hampshire was marked by what he felt was an increasingly pathological aversion to pavement.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Middle English bosk, variant of bush.

words.definition: To remove vulgarities from a work, such as a book or play.
words.example: The popular Kidzbop albums present versions of today's radio tunes featuring bowdlerized lyrics so that the young voices are not reciting age-inappropriate acts to their equally young fans.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from the name of Thomas Bowdler, who published an expurgated edition of Shakespeare in 1818.

words.definition: To squabble.
words.example: An off-handed statement about the actress quickly turned into a vicious brabble about her politics and personal life.
words.origin: Late 15th century: probably from Dutch brabbelen 'to quarrel, jabber'.

words.definition: The annoying or exaggerated talk of someone who is trying to sound very proud or brave.
words.example: His braggadocio hid the fact that he really felt like a kid in a suit that was two sizes too big.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Braggadocchio, a boastful character in Spenser's The Faerie Queene.

words.definition: Vigor or vivacity of style or performance.
words.example: He showed such brio on karaoke night that his friends wondered why he didn't have an album contract.
words.origin: Mid 18th century: from Italian brio 'vivacity', perhaps via Spanish and Celtic origin.

words.definition: A low, muffled sound like distant thunder heard in certain seismic regions, especially along seacoasts and over lakes and thought to be caused by feeble earth tremors.
words.example: He sat watching the water, his dread enhanced by drone of the brontide.
words.origin: Late 19th century: from Greek brontē 'thunder'.

words.definition: Uproar; hubbub.
words.example: The coach caused quite a brouhaha when he suspended one of his star players before the bowl game.
words.origin: Late 19th century: from French, probably imitative of a confused din.

words.definition: A report or rumor.
words.example: The bruit spread like wildfire and destroyed the young politician's reputation and political aspirations.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French bruit 'noise', from bruire 'to roar'.

words.definition: Abrupt or offhand in speech or manner.
words.example: His brusque manner gave him a reputation for coldness and insensitivity.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from French brusque 'lively, fierce', from Italian brusco 'sour'.

words.definition: Of or relating to the pleasant aspects of the countryside and country life.
words.example: Sitting on the subway, surrounded by angry city folk, Jack felt a twinge of longing for his bucolic childhood on the farm.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: via Latin from Greek boukolikos, from boukolos 'herdsman'.

words.definition: An imaginary goblin or specter used to excite fear; an object or source of dread; a continuing source of irritation.
words.example: The bugbear of "weapons of mass destruction" was a mainstay in the Bush administration's media control arsenal.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from obsolete bug 'bogeyman' + bear.

words.definition: Umbrella; parasol.
words.example: Remember to take your bumbershoot on rainy days.
words.origin: Late 19th century (US): a fanciful alteration of umbrella, with chute substituted for the final syllable.

words.definition: To create a fictitious scenario that provides an excuse for avoiding unwanted engagements.
words.example: He declined the invitation to the party by saying he had to help his cousin move, a bunbury he kept in his back pocket even though he hadn't spoken to the relative in decades.
words.origin: Late 19th century: from the fictitious invalid Bunbury in Oscar Wilde's The Importance of Being Earnest (1895).

words.definition: Rubbish; nonsense; empty or misleading talk.
words.example: At last the election year's great festival of buncombe has dimmed to a trickle of murmurs and muttering.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from Buncombe County in North Carolina, mentioned in a rambling speech by its congressman.

words.definition: Cheerful and lighthearted.
words.example: The sunny skies and warm breeze put him in a buoyant mood that afternoon.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Spanish boyante, present participle of boyar 'to float', from boya 'a buoy'.

words.definition: All the things of a group.
words.example: After parsing through all of his baseball cards, he decided to sell the whole caboodle.
words.origin: Mid 19th century (US): probably from the phrase the whole kit and boodle, from boodle 'lot, pile'.

words.definition: To laugh loudly.
words.example: The kids were supposed to be in bed, but I could hear them cachinnating down the hall well past midnight.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from Latin cachinnare 'to laugh loudly', of imitative origin.

words.definition: An irresistible urge to do something inadvisable.
words.example: My commitment to weight loss was derailed by my cacoethes to eat the entire pizza.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: via Latin from Greek kakoēthes, from kakos 'bad' + ēthos 'disposition'.

words.definition: To obtain by wit or cajolery; to mooch.
words.example: By dropping by his old roommate's home around dinner time, Stu hoped to cadge a meal.
words.origin: Early 19th century: back-formation from cadger 'itinerant dealer', of uncertain origin.

words.definition: Partnership, collusion or collaboration, often with nefarious implications.
words.example: Fiona's covert winks made Gavin wonder uneasily if they were supposed to be in cahoots about something he'd forgotten but didn't want anyone to find out.
words.origin: Early 19th century (US): perhaps from French cahute 'cabin, hut'.

words.definition: A noisy or boisterous band or parade.
words.example: It was hard for the bar patrons to talk to each over over the screaming of the callithumps on stage that night.
words.origin: Early 19th century (US): a fanciful formation, perhaps influenced by dialect gallithumpian 'disturber of order'.

words.definition: Immature or lacking adult sophistication.
words.example: He is a brilliant neurologist but callow toward his friends and fellow doctors.
words.origin: Old English calu 'bald', later 'unfledged', of Germanic origin.

words.definition: The making of false and defamatory statements in order to damage someone's reputation; a false and slanderous statement.
words.example: Knowing that his words would damage his nemesis' reputation, Carl engaged in a long-winded embellishment of wrongs that bordered on calumny.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin calumnia, from calvi 'to deceive'.

words.definition: A group of unofficial advisers to someone in authority, often given to scheming or to secret plots; a cabal.
words.example: Four days later I appealed to Hitler again ... about the camarilla in my Ministry that was undermining my program. (Albert Speer, "Inside the Third Reich")
words.origin: Early 19th century: from Spanish camarilla 'little room', diminutive of camara 'chamber'.

words.definition: An unfounded rumor or story.
words.example: He sat at the end of the bar, telling an old canard about the haunted hotel down the block.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from French canard 'duck', from the phrase vendre un canard a moitie 'half-sell a duck', i.e. to swindle.

words.definition: Cranky; disagreeable to deal with; stubborn; contentious; surly.
words.example: Working on his antique tractor always made Jethro's pa as cantankerous as a water moccasin.
words.origin: Mid 18th century: perhaps from a blend of Anglo-Irish cant 'auction' and rancorous.

words.definition: Causing distress or worry.
words.example: His carking thoughts crept in and kept him awake all night.
words.origin: Middle English: from Anglo-Norman French carkier 'to load', from late Latin carcare 'to load a wagon'.

words.definition: A conundrum, nonsensical question or pun.
words.example: He left the kindergarten classroom in a daze, the students' barrage of carriwitchets still echoing in his head.
words.origin: Early 17th century: of unknown origin, originally meaning a quibble or absurd question.

words.definition: Askew or awry.
words.example: When the tornado hit, it left the mailbox completely catawampus.
words.origin: Mid 19th century (US): a fanciful formation, perhaps from cater 'diagonally' + an arbitrary ending.

words.definition: A shrill howling or wailing noise.
words.example: As the storm raged on, the caterwaul from the wind as it whipped through the trees kept me from getting any sleep.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from cater (perhaps imitative of a cat) + waul 'to wail', of imitative origin.

words.definition: Make petty or unnecessary objections.
words.example: Tara always cringed when going into a meeting with Margaret, who would likely cavil for hours over every suggestion she brought up.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from French caviller, from Latin cavillari, from cavilla 'mockery'.

words.definition: The blue of the sky.
words.example: Her eyes were a clear, deep cerulean blue, like no eyes Trevor had ever seen, and looking into them made him feel lighter than air, as though he could fly, but even if he could have flown he would have stayed where he was, content just to look.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin caeruleus 'sky-blue', from caelum 'sky'.

words.definition: Distress or embarrassment at having failed or been humiliated.
words.example: Much to his chagrin, Jack learned during the spelling bee that he had spelled the word "eleven" wrong his entire life.
words.origin: Late 17th century: from French chagrin 'rough skin, sorrow', of unknown ultimate origin.

words.definition: Quack. Imposter.
words.example: This guy claims his anti-aging cream really works, but I think he's just a charlatan.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from French, from Italian ciarlatano, from ciarlare 'to babble'.

words.definition: Miserly economizing.
words.example: My father was so accustomed to cheeseparing that rather than turn up the furnace, he'd make us don two layers of clothing and wear coats inside during the winter.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from cheese + paring, alluding to the meanness of saving parings of cheese rind.

words.definition: Like a turtle (and who doesn't like turtles?).
words.example: Weighed down by bickering and blather, the highway bill crept through Congress at a chelonian pace.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from modern Latin Chelonia, from Greek khelōnē 'tortoise'.

words.definition: Deception; trickery.
words.example: Promoting flooded lowlands unsuitable for building as "prime waterfront real estate" was the type of chicanery for which Mr. Wilson was famous.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from French chicanerie, from chicaner 'to quibble'.

words.definition: A thing that is hoped or wished for but in fact is illusory or impossible to achieve.
words.example: He wanted to be an NFL quarterback, but due to his size and clumsiness, that dream remained a chimera.
words.origin: Late Middle English: via Latin from Greek khimaira 'she-goat, monster', a fire-breathing creature of Greek mythology.

words.definition: Laugh in a breathy, gleeful way; chuckle.
words.example: He was the king of dad jokes, and chortled at every pun he encountered.
words.origin: Coined by Lewis Carroll in 'Through the Looking-Glass' (1871), probably a blend of 'chuckle' and 'snort'.

words.definition: Rude. Boorish. Ill-mannered.
words.example: It was very churlish of you to throw your coffee mug at me.
words.origin: Old English ceorlisc 'of a churl, low-born', from ceorl 'peasant'.

words.definition: Cautious, prudent, wary; taking all circumstances into account.
words.example: Given the presence of Union artillery, Gen. Jackson's Confederates were particularly circumspect as they approached Winchester that morning in 1862.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin circumspectus, from circumspicere 'look around', from circum 'around' and specere 'look'.

words.definition: Absurd or nonsensical talk or ideas.
words.example: His opinion pieces were a rambling, incoherent bit of claptrap.
words.origin: Early 18th century: originally a theatrical term for a trick or device to 'trap' applause, from clap and trap.

words.definition: To split or penetrate; to cling or be faithful.
words.example: He cleaved the rock in two with the sledgehammer. ... OR ... Therefore shall a man leave his father and his mother, and shall cleave unto his wife: and they shall be one flesh. (Genesis 2:24)
words.origin: Old English cleofan 'split' and clifian 'adhere', two distinct verbs of Germanic origin that converged in form.

words.definition: Mild, beautiful weather
words.example: In the midst of winter, I look forward to May, when I can picnic and socialize with friends in clement weather.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin clemens 'mild, merciful'.

words.definition: Unpleasantly excessive; excessively sweet.
words.example: That pecan praline was so cloying on so many levels that I swear I'll never have another as long as I live.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from obsolete cloy 'to glut, satiate', from Old French encloyer 'drive a nail into'.

words.definition: The union (as of dissimilar substances) in one body or mass.
words.example: While the ingredients weren't appetizing on their own, mixing them together created a pleasant coadunation that was the hit of the potluck.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin coadunatio, from coadunare 'to unite', from co- 'together' and adunare 'to join into one'.

words.definition: To walk without a specific destination.
words.example: Donald found that he had no useful purpose that day and coddiwompled about town instead.
words.origin: A modern coinage of uncertain origin, often described as old English slang for travelling purposefully toward a vague destination.

words.definition: Nonsense.
words.example: When I asked where he'd been, he fed me a bunch of codswallop about his car's history of problems.
words.origin: 1960s British slang of unknown origin, popularized through the TV series 'Steptoe and Son'.

words.definition: Convincing, plausible, reasonable, persuasive.
words.example: He presented a cogent argument that acid rain has killed off thousands of acres of U.S. forest.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin cogent-, cogere 'compel', from co- 'together' and agere 'drive'.

words.definition: A person who has superior knowledge or understanding in a particular field.
words.example: His knowledge of the fine arts made him a cognoscente respected in trade journals.
words.origin: Late 18th century: from Italian, literally 'a person who knows', from Latin cognoscere 'to know'.

words.definition: Intense anxiety or nervousness, especially with stomach queasiness.
words.example: While he was confident in rehearsal, performing before a crowd sent him running to the restroom with a case of the collywobbles.
words.origin: Early 19th century: a fanciful formation perhaps based on colic and wobble.

words.definition: An item of food.
words.example: I remember Thanksgiving in my youth, when the table sagged under the weight of all the comestibles.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Old French, from medieval Latin comestibilis, from Latin comest- 'eaten up', from comedere 'eat up'.

words.definition: Courtesy and considerate behavior toward others.
words.example: A woman on the train showed comity by offering her seat to a fellow rider using a cane.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin comitas, from comis 'courteous'.

words.definition: Reduced to minute particles; pulverized.
words.example: I ran into the room to see my son standing by the tree, the ornament that had been passed down for decades comminuted on the hard floor.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin comminut-, comminuere 'break into pieces', from com- and minuere 'lessen'.

words.definition: To behave in a particular way.
words.example: The neighbor's children comported themselves with unexpected good manners.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Latin comportare 'bring together', from com- 'together' and portare 'carry'.

words.definition: A feeling of guilt or moral scruple that prevents or follows the doing of something bad.
words.example: He unleashed the flurry of tweets with no compunction about the bile he spewed.
words.origin: Middle English: from Old French componction, from ecclesiastical Latin compunctio, from compungere 'prick sharply'.

words.definition: A series of interconnected or interdependent things; a chain of events or phenomena.
words.example: For 2,000 years, would-be prophets have delighted in pointing out concatenations of events that supposedly predict the Apocalypse.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from late Latin concatenatio, from concatenare 'link together', from con- and catena 'chain'.

words.definition: The skillful and harmonious arrangement or fitting together of the different parts of something.
words.example: As the choir sang in the vast cathedral, I couldn't help but marvel at the concinnity of Handel's "Messiah."
words.origin: Late 15th century: from Latin concinnitas, from concinnus 'skilfully put together'.

words.definition: Agreement or harmony between people or groups.
words.example: Despite the disagreements that tore the community apart a generation ago, the townsfolk currently live in concord.
words.origin: Middle English: from Old French concorde, from Latin concordia, from concors 'of one mind', from con- and cor 'heart'.

words.definition: Sexual desire or longing; lust.
words.example: Too many political figures, drunk on power and the heady liquor of self-esteem, let concupiscence get the best of them.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from late Latin concupiscentia, from Latin concupiscere 'begin to desire', from con- and cupere 'to desire'.

words.definition: Engage in conversation; talk.
words.example: As he walked into the conference's main room, he could hear the hushed murmur of his peers confabulating with one another.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from Latin confabulat-, confabulari 'chat together', from con- and fabulari 'to talk', from fabula 'tale'.

words.definition: To make something unclear or confusing.
words.example: He had a tendency to confusticate the simplest problems.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: a fanciful elaboration of confuse, popularized by J. R. R. Tolkien in 'The Hobbit'.

words.definition: A fit of extreme anger or excitement.
words.example: My father had a conniption when he found out I totaled his car.
words.origin: Mid 19th century (originally US): of unknown origin, perhaps a fanciful coinage.

words.definition: To deliver,assign or send, usually in a decisive, irrevocable manner.
words.example: The disobedient child was consigned to his room for the rest of the afternoon.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from French consigner or Latin consignare 'mark with a seal', from con- and signum 'sign'.

words.definition: Stunning or confounding amazement and/or dismay.
words.example: Much to my consternation I realized my vocabulary needed to be enlivened.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from French consternation, from Latin consternatio, from consternare 'to dismay'.

words.definition: A walk, typically one taken regularly to maintain or restore good health.
words.example: To improve his cardiovascular health, Dennis set out each weeknight for a post-dinner constitutional.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from constitution (in the sense 'physical health'), the walk being taken for the benefit of one's constitution.

words.definition: A difficult problem to be solved, especially one whose answer involves a pun or play on words. A difficult decision.
words.example: Students in his ethics class were routinely asked to puzzle over moral conundrums.
words.origin: Late 16th century: of unknown origin, originally Oxford University slang denoting a pedant or a whim.

words.definition: Friendly, sociable and lively.
words.example: "One does not leave a convivial party before closing time." - Winston Churchill
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin convivialis, from convivium 'a feast', from con- 'with' and vivere 'live'.

words.definition: Acceptable. Satisfactory.
words.example: Everything was copacetic until I told the truth.
words.origin: 1920s (originally US): of unknown origin.

words.definition: Abundant in supply or quantity.
words.example: After supplying the kids with a copious amount of soda, their grandparents were more than happy to release them back to mom and dad's care.
words.origin: Middle English: from Old French copieux or Latin copiosus, from copia 'plenty'.

words.definition: An excellent or astonishing person or thing.
words.example: Every episode was good, but the finale was quite a corker.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: probably from cork, perhaps in the sense 'something that finishes off' a discussion.

words.definition: Having a large bulky body.
words.example: I moved my corpulent frame up the next flight of stairs, swearing under heavy breath that I would never have another Big Mac.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin corpulentus, from corpus 'body'.

words.definition: (Of light) to flash or sparkle.
words.example: The water was still that afternoon, the sunlight coruscating off what little waves existed.
words.origin: Early 18th century: from Latin coruscat-, coruscare 'to glitter'.

words.definition: Wild; frenzied.
words.example: The wedding reception was two hours of corybantic dancing.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Corybant, a priest of the Phrygian goddess Cybele who performed wild, ecstatic dances.

words.definition: A small group of people with shared interests or tastes, especially one that is exclusive of other people.
words.example: He retreated to an online chat room, where he could be surrounded by a coterie of fellow enthusiasts.
words.origin: Early 18th century: from French, originally denoting an association of tenants, from Old French cotier 'cottager'.

words.definition: A swift horse.
words.example: Jack came into a small fortune by betting his life savings on an unlikely courser.
words.origin: Middle English: from Old French cors(i)er, based on Latin cursarius, from cursus 'course', from currere 'to run'.

words.definition: Cultured, refined and well-mannered.
words.example: Her couth delivery was a relief following the blithering performance of her predecessor.
words.origin: 1890s: a back-formation from uncouth, taken to imply the opposite quality.

words.definition: A conceited foolish person.
words.example: The CEO was a coxcomb who didn't realize his gibberish pronouncements were snickered at by his employees.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: variant of cockscomb, alluding to the cap worn by a professional fool resembling a cock's comb.

words.definition: Exceptionally good.
words.example: He avoided being late only because he found a crackerjack cabbie able to get him to the airport in record time.
words.origin: Late 19th century (originally US): probably from cracker (a person or thing of marked quality) and the suffix -jack.

words.definition: Sick from eating or drinking too much.
words.example: After the excesses of Paczki Day, George woke up feeling both foolish and crapulent.
words.origin: Late 17th century: from late Latin crapulentus 'very drunk', from crapula 'inebriation', from Greek kraipalē 'drunken headache'.

words.definition: Of, relating to, or resembling twilight.
words.example: Our last night at the farm, we watched the fireflies as the crepuscular rays filtered through the trees.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin crepusculum 'twilight'.

words.definition: Disappointed, blue, dejected, depressed.
words.example: After his divorce, Jeremy was crestfallen for so long that he exhausted our sympathy for him.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from crest and fallen, originally describing a cock with a drooping comb after defeat.

words.definition: Originally a container in which substances could be heated to very high temperatures. More commonly now, it means a severe test or trial.
words.example: Great leaders are often formed in the crucible of personal struggles.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from medieval Latin crucibulum 'night lamp, melting pot', perhaps from Latin crux 'cross'.

words.definition: The pivotal point in an argument. From the Latin "crux," or cross.
words.example: My contribution was simply the crux of the matter and left no room for further discussion.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from Latin crux 'cross', used figuratively for a puzzle or difficulty.

words.definition: Blue, like the color of the sea.
words.example: He laid back on the sand and stared out at the placid, cumatical horizon.
words.origin: From Latin cymaticus, relating to waves, ultimately from Greek kuma 'wave'.

words.definition: Eager or excessive desire, especially to possess something; greed; avarice.
words.example: Cupidity often leads people to take things that do not belong to them.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French cupidite or Latin cupiditas, from cupidus 'desirous', from cupere 'to desire'.

words.definition: An aggressive dog or one that is in poor condition, especially a mongrel.
words.example: The dog was a mangy, unpleasant cur who snarled if you got within five feet of him.
words.origin: Middle English (originally in cur-dog): probably from Old Norse kurr 'grumbling'.

words.definition: The shock felt when one first plunges into cold water.
words.example: He dove into the pool without thinking and the curglaff caused him to shriek when he came up for air.
words.origin: 18th century Scots dialect word for the shock of cold water on the skin.

words.definition: A person or thing that is the center of attention or admiration.
words.example: She was the cynosure of every person in the room.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from French cynosure or Latin cynosura, from Greek kunosoura 'dog's tail', also denoting Ursa Minor (the Pole Star).

words.definition: Act or move slowly.
words.example: Every morning, my kid dallies until we're in a mad rush to meet the bus.
words.origin: Middle English (in the sense 'to converse'): from Old French dalier 'to chat'.

words.definition: Move (a baby or young child) up and down in a playful or affectionate way.
words.example: My niece squeals in delight when I dandle her upon my knee.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: of unknown origin; compare with French dandiner 'to waddle' and Italian dandola 'a toy'.

words.definition: Cowardly; meanly base; sneaking.
words.example: "I ask that the Congress declare that since the unprovoked and dastardly attack by Japan on Sunday, December 7th, 1941, a state of war has existed between the United States and the Japanese empire." (President Franklin D. Roosevelt)
words.origin: Late 16th century: from the noun dastard 'a despicable coward', perhaps from Middle English dasart 'a dullard'.

words.definition: Waste time; be slow.
words.example: Not wanting to be cooped up on such a beautiful day, Jack dawdled to work, stopping to admire every garden he passed.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: perhaps related to dialect daddle 'to totter', of uncertain origin.

words.definition: (dey bah' kul) Complete failure; rout; an event that ends in utter disaster.
words.example: After the debacle at Fredericksburg, the Union army regrouped under a new commander. ... OR ... The 2001 Enron debacle was the largest bankruptcy in U.S. history at that time.
words.origin: Early 19th century: from French débâcle, from débâcler 'to unleash', from dé- 'off' and bâcler 'to bar'.

words.definition: Downward inclination; slope.
words.example: The soft declivities of Camilla's neck seemed more like the terrains of paradise, a land in which Brian felt he could be happy forever.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin declivitas, from declivis 'sloping down', from de- 'down' and clivus 'a slope'.

words.definition: Publicly denounce.
words.example: In his press conference, the mayor decried the treasurer's actions.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from French décrier, from Old French descrier 'cry down'.

words.definition: To throw out of a window.
words.example: Bob threatened to defenestrate his laptop if it didn't stop eating his data.
words.origin: Late 19th century: a back-formation from defenestration, from Latin de- 'down from' and fenestra 'window'.

words.definition: Taste (something) carefully, so as to appreciate it fully.
words.example: He savored the meal, pausing to degust every morsel.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin degustare, from de- 'down' and gustare 'to taste'.

words.definition: Pleasure and delight.
words.example: I showed up with a box of chocolates for her delectation.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French, or from Latin delectatio(n-), from delectare 'to charm'.

words.definition: Criticize; defame; disparage. cause to seem less serious; play down
words.example: My professor dislikes me so much that he pounces on chances to denigrate my writing. ... OR ... Katy's joking reaction seemed to denigrate the seriousness of the situation.
words.origin: Late Middle English (in the sense 'blacken, make dark'): from Latin denigrat- 'blackened', from the verb denigrare, from de- 'away, completely' + niger 'black'.

words.definition: The final part of a play, movie, or narrative in which the strands of the plot are drawn together and matters are explained or resolved.
words.example: The film was gripping for 90 minutes and then fell apart at the rushed denouement.
words.origin: Mid 18th century: French dénouement, from dénouer 'unknot', from des- (expressing reversal) + nouer 'to knot' (from Latin nodus 'knot').

words.definition: Ridiculously small or inadequate.
words.example: For the outlandish price that we paid, the restaurant provided derisory servings of food.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin derisor 'mocker' (from deridere 'scoff at') + -y.

words.definition: Things that are required or wanted.
words.example: The committee met to discuss the various desiderata of a successful village fete.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: Latin, plural of desideratum, neuter past participle of desiderare 'desire'.

words.definition: (Des' wi tyood). Obsolescence; a state of disuse.
words.example: Knowing the regrettable desuetude of manners today, I wasn't surprised that no one thanked me for their gifts.
words.origin: Late 15th century: from French désuétude or Latin desuetudo, from desuet- 'made unaccustomed', from the verb desuescere.

words.definition: Instructive; intending to teach (adj.)
words.example: The manner in which he presented his speech was less persuasive than didactic.
words.origin: Late 17th century: from Greek didaktikos, from didaskein 'teach'.

words.definition: Modesty brought on by a lack of self-confidence.
words.example: Gary Cooper's portrayal of a diffident Will Kane in "High Noon" was worthy of his Oscar win.
words.origin: Late Middle English (in the sense 'lacking confidence or trust in someone or something'): from Latin diffident- 'failing in trust', from the verb diffidere, from dis- (expressing reversal) + fidere 'to trust'.

words.definition: Dress, adorn.
words.example: The knight barely had time to dight his armor as he rushed out the door to join the others in the quest.
words.origin: Old English dihtan 'to dictate', also 'compose, arrange', from Latin dictare 'dictate, prescribe'.

words.definition: A person who cultivates an area of interest, such as the arts, without real commitment or knowledge.
words.example: He was more a dilettante than a critic, able to tell you that a band sounded "cool," but unable to recognize the foundation the music was built on.
words.origin: Mid 18th century: from Italian, 'lover of the arts', from dilettare 'to delight', from Latin delectare.

words.definition: A loud, unpleasant, and prolonged noise.
words.example: His ears rang from the din of the stadium crowd.
words.origin: Old English dyne, dynn (noun), dynian (verb), of Germanic origin.

words.definition: A mournful song, piece of music or poem.
words.example: He stood at the bar, drink in hand, singing a traditional Irish dirge.
words.origin: Middle English: from Latin dirige! 'direct!' (imperative of dirigere), the first word of an antiphon used in the Office for the Dead.

words.definition: To free someone from a misconception.
words.example: I'm afraid I must disabuse you of the notion that chitterlings are tasty.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from French désabuser, from dés- (expressing reversal) + abuser 'deceive'.

words.definition: The removal of ambiguity; clarification.
words.example: Before I could answer his question, his confusing use of the word "bass" required some disambiguation.
words.origin: Early 19th century: from dis- (expressing reversal) + ambiguity, from Latin ambiguus 'doubtful'.

words.definition: Confuse or upset.
words.example: He was discombobulated by his wife's moodiness.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: American English, probably a fanciful alteration of discompose or discomfit.

words.definition: Lacking in candor; insincere.
words.example: Her reasons for not joining us were plainly disingenuous, so I ignored them.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from dis- (expressing reversal) + ingenuous, from Latin ingenuus 'native, inborn'.

words.definition: To be indecisive.
words.example: I hated going out to eat with Jack, who would spend a half hour dithering over what to order.
words.origin: Late Middle English didder 'tremble, quiver'; the current sense dates from the early 19th century.

words.definition: Verse or words that are badly written or expressed.
words.example: His novel was nothing more than 200 pages of clumsy, pretentious doggerel.
words.origin: Middle English (as an adjective): apparently from dog (used contemptuously) + -rel.

words.definition: A shapeless mass or blob of something, especially soft food.
words.example: He sat down to enjoy a nice steak, grilled asparagus and a baked potato topped with a giant dollop of sour cream.
words.origin: Late 16th century (denoting a lump or clump of something): perhaps of Scandinavian or Low German origin.

words.definition: A small object or gadget, especially one whose name the speaker does not know or cannot recall.
words.example: The mechanic came out, fiddled about the engine with his doohickey, and then charged me $2,000.
words.origin: Early 20th century: probably a blend of doodad and hickey.

words.definition: In literature and film, a ghostly double or spirit, often presaging evil; in daily use, more commonly a physical double or look-alike. From the German Doppel ("double") and Ganger ("goer").
words.example: After their prickly breakup, Edmund was disturbed to see doppelgangers of Elizabeth everywhere he went.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from German Doppelgänger, literally 'double-goer'.

words.definition: Brave and persistent.
words.example: Despite the obstacles, the doughty explorers kept on and eventually reached the summit.
words.origin: Old English dohtig, variant of dyhtig, of Germanic origin; related to German tüchtig.

words.definition: Hard, severe, cruel. From Draco, a politician of ancient Athens whose codified laws were notorious for their severity, such as death for minor offenses.
words.example: I hear the government is considering draconian penalties for those who leak classified documents.
words.origin: Mid 18th century: from Draco, a 7th-century BC Athenian legislator whose laws were notoriously harsh, + -ian.

words.definition: To compel by coercion; to force someone to do something they'd rather not.
words.example: After working in the yard all day, Bubba was dragooned into going to the ballet instead of flopping down to watch the Red Wings on TV.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from French dragon 'dragon, carbine, mounted infantryman', the verb sense from the persecution of French Protestants by mounted soldiers.

words.definition: A fit or state of indignation.
words.example: The council member watched as his proposals were rejected one by one and then stormed off in high dudgeon.
words.origin: Late 16th century: of unknown origin.

words.definition: Pleasing to the ear or sweet to the taste.
words.example: Instead of yelling at us to be quiet, my father urged us to "use dulcet tones!"
words.origin: Late Middle English doucet, from Old French doucet, diminutive of doux 'sweet', from Latin dulcis; later altered to conform with the Latin.

words.definition: The state of finding it hard to get out of bed in the morning.
words.example: On Mondays, my dysania can cause me to reach for the snooze alarm upwards of five times.
words.origin: Modern coinage, from Greek dys- 'difficulty' + anistasthai 'to rise'.

words.definition: Bursting with great enthusiasm or excitement.
words.example: Jane was always so ebullient that just being around her for two minutes made me want to take a nap.
words.origin: Late 16th century (in the sense 'boiling'): from Latin ebullient- 'boiling up', from the verb ebullire, from e- 'out' + bullire 'to boil'.

words.definition: Producing or capable of producing a desired effect.
words.example: Whether sincere or not, praise is often the most efficacious way to acquire people's loyalty.
words.origin: Early 16th century: from Latin efficax, efficac- (from efficere 'accomplish') + -ious.

words.definition: A slight or invisible exhalation or vapor, esp. one that is disagreeable or foul-smelling.
words.example: The landfill's effluvium seemed to waft inexorably toward the town.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin, from effluere 'to flow out', from ex- 'out' + fluere 'to flow'.

words.definition: Shameless audacity; rank impudence.
words.example: When he advised his listeners to ignore segregationist ordinances, Martin Luther King was vilified by some for having the effrontery to suggest which laws should be obeyed.
words.origin: Early 18th century: from French effronterie, based on late Latin effrons, effront- 'shameless', from ex- 'out' + frons 'forehead'.

words.definition: 1. Shining brightly; radiant. 2. (Of a person or their expression) emanating joy or goodness.
words.example: Her beauty was enhanced by her effulgent personality.
words.origin: Mid 18th century: from Latin effulgent- 'shining brightly', from the verb effulgere, from ex- 'out' + fulgere 'to shine'.

words.definition: Outstanding, usually in the negative sense. Outrageously bad.
words.example: I made an egregious mistake in calling Madeline her mother when she is in fact her older sister.
words.origin: Mid 16th century (in the sense 'remarkably good'): from Latin egregius 'illustrious', literally 'standing out from the flock', from ex- 'out' + grex, greg- 'flock'.

words.definition: Manage to support oneself or make a living with difficulty.
words.example: He eked out enough of a living through delivery and other gigs to barely pay his rent.
words.origin: Old English eaca 'an increase', eacian 'to increase'; the phrase 'eke out' dates from the late 16th century.

words.definition: Energy, style and enthusiasm.
words.example: The band's performance was filled with elan and kept the audience moving.
words.origin: Late 19th century: from French, from élancer 'to dart', from é- 'out' + lancer 'to throw'.

words.definition: Eerie, spooky, uncomfortably weird.
words.example: The feeble light of the waning moon, the crumbling stones, the dark shadows of skeletal trees and the mournful cries of owls gave the old cemetery such an eldritch aspect that we got out of there as fast as we could.
words.origin: Early 16th century: perhaps related to elf, and an Old English second element meaning 'foreign, strange'.

words.definition: Charitable.
words.example: Ebenezer Scrooge was transformed from a "tight-fisted hand to the grindstone" into an eleemosynary gentleman that kept Christmas in his heart all year round.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from medieval Latin eleemosynarius, from late Latin eleemosyna 'alms', from Greek eleemosune 'compassion'.

words.definition: A poem of serious reflection, typically a lament for the dead.
words.example: The elegy, written for the father he never met, was published two years after his death.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from French élégie, or via Latin from Greek elegeia, from elegos 'mournful poem'.

words.definition: Hair matted as if by elves.
words.example: She woke up in the morning with her hair knotted in elflocks.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from elf + lock, from the belief that elves tangled the hair of sleepers.

words.definition: To weaken (reduce in strength) or debilitate.
words.example: After gardening in the hot sun for three hours, I was so enervated I had to take a nap.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin enervat- 'weakened (by extraction of the sinews)', from the verb enervare, from e- 'out' + nervus 'sinew'.

words.definition: Boredom. Lack of interest.
words.example: It was a difficult year for Sigmund, and his paralyzing ennui had returned with a vengeance.
words.origin: Mid 18th century: French, from Latin in odio in the phrase mihi in odio est 'it is hateful to me'.

words.definition: Heinous, horrible or monstrous in quality or character; extremely wicked.
words.example: Jefferson's apparent acceptance of the enormity of the French Revolution's excesses greatly widened the gulf between him and Hamilton.
words.origin: Late Middle English (in the sense 'deviation from a normal standard'): via Old French from Latin enormitas, from enormis, from e- 'out of' + norma 'pattern, standard'.

words.definition: Enchanted or bewitched.
words.example: He was ensorcelled by her delicious twice-baked potatoes.
words.origin: Late 15th century: from Old French ensorceler, alteration of ensorcerer, from sorcier 'sorcerer'.

words.definition: Lasting or existing briefly.
words.example: She treasured those ephemeral moments of joy, knowing they would soon be lost to the routine of everyday life.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Greek ephemeros 'lasting only a day', from epi 'on' + hemera 'day'.

words.definition: A less distinguished follower or imitator of someone, especially an artist or philosopher.
words.example: Even their most loyal fans knew The Monkees were a silly, manufactured epigone of The Beatles.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from Greek epigonoi 'those born afterwards', plural of epigonos, from epi 'in addition' + the root of gignesthai 'be born'.

words.definition: Mental calmness, composure and evenness of temper, especially in a difficult situation.
words.example: It will take an extra measure of equanimity to stay the course and reach my goal amid the chaos of these times.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin aequanimitas, from aequus 'equal' + animus 'mind'.

words.definition: To speak ambiguously or evasively, in such a way as to avoid taking a position; hedge.
words.example: Respondents in Senate hearings on the oil spill preferred to equivocate, their answers all scuttling sideways, like crabs.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from late Latin aequivocat- 'called by the same name', from the verb aequivocare, from aequivocus, from aequus 'equally' + vocare 'to call'.

words.definition: A person who deeply enjoys working and possesses a special love, passion or desire for work.
words.example: Ever the ergophile, she was always the first in the office and last to leave, and a smile rarely left her face.
words.origin: Modern coinage, from Greek ergon 'work' + -phile 'lover of'.

words.definition: Latin word meaning an error. The plural is "errata."
words.example: You need to recheck this report as it contains one erratum after another.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Latin, neuter past participle of errare 'to err'.

words.definition: A substitute, usually inferior; artificial in a pejorative sense.
words.example: Walter thought his stories were pretty good, but to the rest of us they were obviously just ersatz Hemingway.
words.origin: Late 19th century: from German Ersatz 'replacement', from ersetzen 'replace'.

words.definition: Avoid; shun.
words.example: An unfortunate allergy means that I have to eschew the pleasure of eating raw oysters.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French eschiver, ultimately of Germanic origin and related to German scheuen 'shun, fear'.

words.definition: Understood, known by or intended for a very few; requiring knowledge possessed by a select group.
words.example: Many readers ignore the esoteric imagery in Flannery O'Connor's fiction and enjoy the stories just as they are.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Greek esoterikos, from esotero, comparative of eso 'within'.

words.definition: A sudden and favorable resolution of events in a story; a happy ending.
words.example: No matter how convoluted the story gets, every romantic comedy ends in a eucatastrophe.
words.origin: Coined by J. R. R. Tolkien in 1944, from Greek eu- 'good' + catastrophe, from katastrophe 'overturning'.

words.definition: Ephemeral; fleeting.
words.example: The younger you are, the more evanescent your dreams of true love tend to be.
words.origin: Early 18th century: from Latin evanescent- 'disappearing', from the verb evanescere, from e- 'out of' + vanus 'empty'.

words.definition: The end of the day; evening.
words.example: I sat on the beach at eventide with my wife and kids and realized just how blessed I was.
words.origin: Old English æfentid, from æfen 'evening' + tid 'time, tide'.

words.definition: ih VISS ur reyt. Verb. Literally, to disembowel. More commonly, to remove vital parts from something or make it virtually meaningless.
words.example: Despite his good intentions, Brendan's bill to limit campaign spending was eviscerated before it reached the floor of the House.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin eviscerat- 'disembowelled', from the verb eviscerare, from e- 'out' + viscera 'internal organs'.

words.definition: Think out, plan or devise.
words.example: The committee sat in the room for hours excogitating a solution.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin excogitat- 'found by process of thought', from the verb excogitare, from ex- 'out' + cogitare 'think'.

words.definition: To rebuke scathingly; to censure; to flay verbally.
words.example: In town hall meetings the mayor was routinely excoriated for neglecting the city's schools.
words.origin: Late Middle English (in the sense 'strip off skin'): from Latin excoriat- 'skinned', from the verb excoriare, from ex- 'out, from' + corium 'skin, hide'.

words.definition: (eks' sek rab bl) Atrocious, wretched, deplorable, extremely inferior.
words.example: We were appalled to discover that even though he had a master's degree, his spelling was execrable.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French, or from Latin execrabilis, from exsecrari 'to curse', from ex- 'out' + sacrare 'devote to' (from sacer 'sacred').

words.definition: Choice, excellent, select or distinguished.
words.example: The bejeweled necklace was an eximious treasure in an already noteworthy collection.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin eximius 'select, distinguished' (from eximere 'take out') + -ous.

words.definition: Something that wakes you up in the morning.
words.example: The expergefactor chirped incessantly in the morning darkness, causing him to curse daylight saving time.
words.origin: From Latin expergefacere 'to awaken', from expergisci 'to wake up' and facere 'to make'.

words.definition: The act of making amends or reparation for guilt or wrongdoing; atonement.
words.example: He showed up with a bundle of flowers as expiation, but it wouldn't be enough to get him off the couch at night.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin expiatio, from expiare 'to atone for', from ex- 'completely' and piare 'appease'.

words.definition: Erase or remove completely (something unwanted or unpleasant).
words.example: After seven years, the multiple traffic tickets he accrued as a teenager were expunged from his record.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin expungere 'mark for deletion by pointing', from ex- 'out' and pungere 'to prick'.

words.definition: Treating serious issues with deliberately inappropriate humor; flippant.
words.example: Even though his doctor warned him about the risks to his health, he brushed it off with a facetious remark and took no heed.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from French facétieux, from facétie, from Latin facetia 'jest'.

words.definition: Appearing neat and comprehensive only by ignoring the true complexities of an issue; superficial.
words.example: His facile economic theories were quickly undone by an army of experts pointing out the nuances of reality.
words.origin: Late 15th century: from French, or from Latin facilis 'easy', from facere 'do, make'.

words.definition: Atrociously wicked; infamous.
words.example: Hannibal Lecter is one of the most facinorous literary characters of the 20th century.
words.origin: Early 16th century: from Latin facinorosus, from facinus 'a deed, crime', from facere 'to do'.

words.definition: A person having many diverse activities or responsibilities.
words.example: My workload was pushed aside for a pile of tedious chores the week the office factotum went on vacation.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from medieval Latin, from Latin fac! 'do!' and totum 'the whole thing'.

words.definition: A state of extreme anxiety, nervousness or distress; the willies to the max.
words.example: Jeremy's love for islands was tempered by the fact that driving over high bridges always gave him the raging fantods.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: of unknown origin, perhaps related to fantasy or fantastic.

words.definition: An assortment or medley, a jumble or a hodgepodge.
words.example: The art exhibit was a farrago of different styles and periods.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin farrago 'mixed fodder, mixture', from far 'corn'.

words.definition: Foolish, silly, inane, insipid.
words.example: Listening to the senator's fatuous remarks, we wondered if he was naive, ignorant -- or perhaps just not interested.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin fatuus 'foolish' and -ous.

words.definition: Ineffectual, ineffective, incompetent, weak.
words.example: After a few feckless attempts to change the tire on his Aston-Martin, Jeremy sighed, called AAA and ate a nice, juicy peach while he waited for help.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Scots and northern English feck (a contraction of effeck, variant of effect) and -less.

words.definition: Intellectually productive or inventive to a marked degree.
words.example: He had a fecund imagination, constantly churning out new books, reports and lectures.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French fecond or Latin fecundus 'fruitful'.

words.definition: Very well suited or expressed.
words.example: In a felicitous turn of events, he found the hundred dollar bill in the laundry just before he ran out to begin Christmas shopping.
words.origin: Late 18th century: from felicity, from Latin felicitas, from felix 'happy'.

words.definition: To bring something to light by searching (usually with "out"); to search for something lost or hidden.
words.example: Despite the councilwoman's rumored liaison with the mayor, we ferreted through her emails for two hours without finding anything incriminating.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French furet, based on Latin fur 'thief'.

words.definition: A knockout blow.
words.example: When Rocky delivered the ferricadouzer to Apollo at the end of "Rocky II," audiences went wild.
words.origin: 19th-century British dialect term of uncertain origin, possibly an alteration of an Italian phrase.

words.definition: Hasty.
words.example: He remembered their anniversary too late and threw together a sloppy and rather festinate celebration at the local Applebee's.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin festinatus, past participle of festinare 'to hasten'.

words.definition: To adorn or decorate, principally in a loop between two points.
words.example: After lunch we decided to festoon the tree with garlands of electric loons, moons, spoons, puccoons, cocoons, bassoons, baboons and vinegaroons.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from French feston, from Italian festone 'festal ornament', from festa 'feast'.

words.definition: Attractive.
words.example: I was captivated by her beauty; she really was quite fetching.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from fetch (in the sense 'to draw, attract') and -ing.

words.definition: Smelling extremely unpleasant.
words.example: They crept into the basement, the fetid odor of mildew and rot growing stronger as they went.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin foetidus, from foetere 'to stink'.

words.definition: To steal secretly or casually.
words.example: While her parents' eyes were on the TV, the toddler crept into the kitchen and filched a cookie.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: of unknown origin, originally thieves' slang.

words.definition: Something that acts as a stimulus or a boost to a situation.
words.example: Their win gave a much-needed fillip to the school's athletics team.
words.origin: Late Middle English: imitative of the sound made by snapping a finger from the thumb.

words.definition: Obtain something by devious or dishonest means.
words.example: Don't ask me how, but I was able to finagle three extra tickets to the concert on Saturday.
words.origin: 1920s: perhaps from dialect fainaigue 'to cheat', of unknown origin.

words.definition: A fight with fists.
words.example: What started as a civil debate soon devolved into fisticuffs in the alley.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from archaic fisty (from fist) and cuff 'a blow'.

words.definition: Nonsense.
words.example: His talk show was a collection of flapdoodle about politics and conspiracies.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: of unknown origin, a fanciful coinage.

words.definition: Excitement associated with a new beginning.
words.example: Students came to campus today fueled by the fleshment that followed a productive first week.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from flesh (in the sense 'to make eager for combat') and -ment; used by Shakespeare in King Lear.

words.definition: A scatterbrained or ditzy person.
words.example: When the latest scandal came to light, the local flibbertigibbets couldn't begin chattering about it fast enough.
words.origin: Late Middle English: probably imitative of meaningless chatter.

words.definition: The action or habit of estimating something as worthless.
words.example: The film was just a 90 minute exercise in floccinaucinihilipilification.
words.origin: Mid 18th century: a jocular coinage combining Latin flocci, nauci, nihili, and pili (all meaning 'of little value') with -fication.

words.definition: Excessive speed or urgency of movement or action.
words.example: The kids spent most of the summer day rotting around the house but moved with a frantic fluckadrift to straighten things up once they knew Mom was on her way home from the office.
words.origin: Regional dialect term of uncertain origin.

words.definition: Bewilder; baffle; confuse.
words.example: To her dismay, Beth was completely flummoxed by the instructions for taking her new birth control pills.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: of unknown origin, perhaps from English dialect.

words.definition: To pass off as genuine or worthy.
words.example: We made our way up the boardwalk, where merchants attempted to foist cheap jewelry and knock-off clothing on unsuspecting tourists.
words.origin: Late 16th century: probably from Dutch dialect vuisten 'take in the hand', from vuist 'fist'.

words.definition: 1. Nonsense. 2. A pretty but useless trinket.
words.example: I listened to both arguments, but the whole discussion was just a lot of folderol to me.
words.origin: Early 18th century: originally a refrain in old songs, of nonsensical origin.

words.definition: Instigate or stir up (an undesirable or violent sentiment or course of action).
words.example: The politician had a knack for fomenting division and unrest.
