words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French fomenter, from late Latin fomentare, from Latin fomentum 'poultice, lotion'.

words.definition: Engage in fruitless activity; mess about.
words.example: He footled around the house aimlessly, ignoring the checklist of chores his wife had left on the fridge.
words.origin: Late 19th century: perhaps from dialect footy 'marshy', or from French se foutre 'to care nothing'.

words.definition: To fumble, bungle or make a mess of.
words.example: I thought that I had the skills to complete that exercise, but instead I completely foozled the whole thing.
words.origin: Late 19th century: perhaps from German dialect fuseln 'to work badly'.

words.definition: Two weeks.
words.example: When I resign, I have to submit a fortnight's notice.
words.origin: Old English feowertyne niht 'fourteen nights'.

words.definition: Cranky. Unruly. Peevish.
words.example: He is an incorrigibly fractious individual, but he has a certain undeniable charm.
words.origin: Late 18th century: from fraction (in the sense 'brawling') and -ous, on the pattern of captious.

words.definition: Fragile; brittle.
words.example: He picked up the frangible remains of the stained-glass display, which promptly fell apart in his hands.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from medieval Latin frangibilis, from Latin frangere 'to break'.

words.definition: As a verb, to exhaust physically or emotionally; as a noun, the state of being exhausted or spent.
words.example: I love New York, but after a week there I'm so frazzled that home looks better than ever.
words.origin: Early 19th century: perhaps a blend of fray and dialect fazle 'to tangle'.

words.definition: An involuntary shiver, often signaling great emotion.
words.example: Albert knew he'd be glad to see Victoria, but he wasn't expecting a powerful frisson of pleasure when he took her hand.
words.origin: Mid 18th century: French, literally 'shiver, thrill', from late Latin frictio(n-) 'rubbing'.

words.definition: Coolness or extreme reserve in manner.
words.example: They used to be best friends, but the argument created a froideur between them when they passed in the hall.
words.origin: Mid 20th century: French, literally 'coldness', from froid 'cold'.

words.definition: Fat and squat.
words.example: The accountant, a fubsy man whose suit was far too tight, squeezed himself into the tiny booth.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from obsolete fubs 'small fat person', perhaps a blend of fat and chub.

words.definition: To pretend to work when in reality one is not doing anything.
words.example: He fudgeled at his desk, a comic book hidden inside his history text.
words.origin: 18th-century English dialect term of uncertain origin.

words.definition: A heavy, stale, suffocating atmosphere; warm, unpleasantly thick, humid air.
words.example: Saying goodbye to the cats, Roger stepped out of his cool house into the fug of August in southern Louisiana.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: perhaps related to fog or fusty.

words.definition: Tending to disappear; fleeting.
words.example: It was only now, as he sat on the cusp of a new year, that he realized how fugacious all of those beautiful moments of the past 12 months were.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin fugax, fugac- 'inclined to flee', from fugere 'to flee', and -ous.

words.definition: To condemn or denounce. Usually followed by "against."
words.example: Beth regularly fulminated against sex education in public schools, yet saw nothing strange in her equal contempt for teen pregnancy.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin fulminat-, from fulminare 'to strike with lightning', from fulmen 'lightning'.

words.definition: Excessively, even offensively, flattering or ingratiating. Insincere. Beware attempts to give this word a positive spin.
words.example: As an art critic, his observations of emerging artists are more fulsome than encouraging.
words.origin: Middle English (in the sense 'abundant'): from full and -some.

words.definition: Attempting to avoid notice or attention, typically because of guilt or a belief that discovery would lead to trouble; secretive.
words.example: He made a furtive escape, hoping none of his colleagues noticed him as he dashed out to the job interview.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from French furtif, furtive, from Latin furtivus, from furtum 'theft'.

words.definition: An excessively picky or fault-finding person.
words.example: Alice, I'm never having lunch with a fussbudget like you again. Even the salt wasn't salty enough for you.
words.origin: Early 20th century: from fuss and budget (in the dialect sense 'a bag of contents').

words.definition: Pompous or pretentious speech or writing.
words.example: His autobiography was a dull fustian that showcased his knowledge but revealed his lack of wisdom.
words.origin: Middle English: from Old French fustian, from medieval Latin fustaneum, perhaps from Fustat, a suburb of Cairo where cloth was made.

words.definition: Smelling stale, damp, or stuffy.
words.example: A fusty odor seeped out from the door of the pantry.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French fuste 'smelling of the cask', from fust 'cask, tree trunk', from Latin fustis 'cudgel'.

words.definition: Fool around; pass time idly and aimlessly.
words.example: Roger had a great day off, futzing with the stuff in his garage till he'd set an enviable standard for the perpetuation of chaos.
words.origin: 1930s: perhaps from Yiddish arumfartzen 'to fart around'.

words.definition: To go around from one place to another in the pursuit of pleasure or entertainment.
words.example: He spent his weekends gadding about town, visiting every pub, theater and party he could find.
words.origin: Late Middle English: probably a back-formation from obsolete gadling 'wanderer, vagabond', from Old English gædeling 'companion'.

words.definition: A habitual pleasure-seeker.
words.example: The wealthy gadabout constantly made tabloid headlines, showing up at the best parties and first to partake in the latest cultural happening.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from the verb gad 'to roam' and about.

words.definition: To deny or contradict.
words.example: Aaron gainsaid Olivia’s explanation, and together they tried to develop a clearer one to resolve their dilemma.
words.origin: Middle English: from obsolete gain- 'against' (from Old Norse gegn 'straight') and say.

words.definition: Hodgepodge, jumble. From a 16th-century French word meaning "stew."
words.example: Every family had a few skeletons in their cupboards, but the Vanger family had an entire gallimaufry of them. (Stieg Larsson, "The Girl With the Dragon Tattoo")
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from archaic French galimafrée 'unappetizing dish', of unknown origin.

words.definition: To frolic or roam about in a joyful manner, with no definite plan or objective.
words.example: We spent a lovely afternoon gallivanting around the amusement park. ... OR ... I don't want you kids gallivanting all over the countryside this afternoon, so stay home!
words.origin: Early 19th century: perhaps a humorous alteration of gallant.

words.definition: A clumsy or oafish person.
words.example: The big galoot knocked over several items as he barreled through the living room.
words.origin: Early 19th century: originally nautical slang for a soldier or marine; of unknown origin.

words.definition: Waterproof shoes or boots. "Galoshes" may be said to be onomatopoeic, mimicking the sound they make when splashing through puddles.
words.example: In rainy weather like this I always wear my galoshes; they may be garish, but they keep my feet dry.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French galoche, from late Latin gallicula, diminutive of Latin gallica (solea) 'Gallic (shoe)'.

words.definition: Move in a clumsy, ponderous or noisy manner.
words.example: Tim found it hard to study as his father galumphed down the hallway outside the door, banging groceries against the wall and knocking over a pile of books on an end table.
words.origin: Late 19th century: coined by Lewis Carroll in 'Through the Looking-Glass', perhaps blending 'gallop' and 'triumph'.

words.definition: Run or jump about playfully.
words.example: After a winter cooped inside the house, the kids gambolled around the park for hours to celebrate spring's return.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from French 'gambade', from Italian 'gambata' 'leg movement', from 'gamba' 'leg'.

words.definition: Given to prosy, rambling or tedious loquacity. Pointlessly or annoyingly talkative.
words.example: The flight home would have provided Max an ideal opportunity to recover from the conference, were he not stuck next to a garrulous salesman who spouted inane trivia about every city they flew over.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin 'garrulus', from 'garrire' 'to chatter'.

words.definition: Boast extravagantly.
words.example: As soon as he received his test scores, he began to gasconade about the results across his social media channels.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from French 'gasconnade', from 'gasconner' 'to boast', from 'Gascon', the people of Gascony being noted for boasting.

words.definition: Lacking ease or grace; unsophisticated and socially awkward
words.example: His gauche demeanor made Tom stand out like a sore thumb in the crowd of New York socialites.
words.origin: Mid 18th century: French, literally 'left, awkward', from 'gauchir' 'turn aside, swerve'.

words.definition: A place of suffering, misery or torment. Biblical reference.
words.example: Jennifer often said her office was a Gehenna of dashed ambitions, futile dreams and unfulfilled potential, but after a couple hours at the Christmas party it seemed pretty cool to me.
words.origin: From Hebrew 'gē-hinnōm' 'valley of Hinnom', a place near Jerusalem associated with refuse-burning and torment.

words.definition: Polite, refined, stylish or graceful in manner.
words.example: Roger felt that the genteel aristocracy of his Southern childhood had been displaced by generations of greedy entrepreneurs with little regard for heritage or tradition.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from French 'gentil' 'well-born', from Latin 'gentilis' 'of the same clan'.

words.definition: Use gestures, especially dramatic ones, instead of speaking or to emphasize one's words.
words.example: He ran to the side of the road and gesticulated wildly to the oncoming drivers to warn them of the dangers ahead.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin 'gesticulat-', from 'gesticulari', from 'gesticulus', diminutive of 'gestus' 'action'.

words.definition: A worthless, showy bauble.
words.example: Don't waste your money on such gaudy gewgaws.
words.origin: Middle English: of unknown origin.

words.definition: 1. Characterized by convexity or swelling, as when the moon is more than half illuminated, but not full. 2. Humped or hunchbacked.
words.example: "The waning gibbous moon shone a ghostly light on the trail through the bare trees."
words.origin: Late Middle English: from late Latin 'gibbosus', from Latin 'gibbus' 'hump'.

words.definition: A perpetually smiling face.
words.example: Even on the hardest day, her gigglemug radiated joy and warmth.
words.origin: 19th-century British slang, from 'giggle' and 'mug' (slang for face).

words.definition: Flimsy or poorly made but deceptively attractive.
words.example: They filled their new house with gimcrack splendor.
words.origin: Late Middle English: probably an alteration of obsolete 'gibecrake', a kind of ornament, of unknown origin.

words.definition: Foolish; giddy.
words.example: He walked around the city for his first time, mesmerized by the lights and signs, a glaikit look on his face.
words.origin: Late Middle English (Scots and northern English): of unknown origin.

words.definition: To make a joke; jest.
words.example: He rarely had anything of substance to say and instead spent most of his time gleeking at others' expense.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French 'glic', name of a card game, of Germanic origin.

words.definition: An angry or sullen look.
words.example: The glower on my wife's face as I surveyed the mess left by our toddler informed me that inquiring about her day would only get me in trouble.
words.origin: Middle English (originally Scots and northern English): perhaps related to Middle Low German 'glūren' 'to leer' and Middle Dutch 'gloeren' 'to leer'.

words.definition: Grumpy, sullen, gloomy.
words.example: The rain of the Monday morning matched his glumpish mood.
words.origin: From 'glump' 'to look sullen', of imitative origin, related to 'glum'.

words.definition: Piece, portion, fragment, especially but not only of meat. More recently, a literary excerpt proposed for analysis, review or discussion.
words.example: "Be suspicious of relics," Siegfried said wryly. "You could build a stadium out of all the gobbets of the True Cross in European churches."
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French 'gobet', diminutive of 'gobe' 'mouthful, lump'.

words.definition: Incomprehensible jargon bordering on gibberish.
words.example: Rather than using long speeches filled with gobbledygook, some lawyers would be better understood if they spoke in layman's terms.
words.origin: 1944: coined by Maury Maverick, US congressman, probably in imitation of a turkey's gobble.

words.definition: A person who enjoys watching boats and activities on canals.
words.example: We walked together by the water, mingling among the gongoozlers as the sun set.
words.origin: Early 20th century: probably from Lincolnshire dialect 'gawn' and 'gooze' 'to stare vacantly'.

words.definition: To paralyze or stupefy.
words.example: Back when I was in school, it took just one dark look from a teacher to gorgonize an entire classroom of restless kids.
words.origin: From 'Gorgon', the snake-haired creatures of Greek mythology whose gaze turned onlookers to stone.

words.definition: Pompous or extravagant in language, style, or manner, especially in a way that is intended to impress.
words.example: His grandiloquent affectations, meant to endear him to the elite, instead made him an object of ridicule.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Latin 'grandiloquus', from 'grandis' 'grand' and 'loqui' 'speak', altered to match 'eloquent'.

words.definition: Technical jargon (as of legal matters).
words.example: He sat on the jury, bored to tears as the lawyers composed paragraph upon paragraph of grimgribber that he couldn't hope to follow.
words.origin: 17th century: of unknown origin, perhaps a fanciful coinage mimicking obscure legal speech.

words.definition: To stare at people who are eating in the hope that they will offer to share their food.
words.example: As the family enjoyed the Thanksgiving feast, the dog groaked from across the room.
words.origin: Of uncertain Scots dialect origin.

words.definition: To feel or grope in the dark.
words.example: She made her way through the pitch-black kitchen and grubbled at the walls for the light switch.
words.origin: 17th century: frequentative of 'grub', meaning to dig or search.

words.definition: To lie close wrapped up and in a comfortable-looking manner; used in ridicule.
words.example: The rest of the family got up and began packing up the rest of the boxes while Jerry lay grufeling in bed, oblivious to their hard work.
words.origin: 18th-century Scots dialect, of obscure origin.

words.definition: A person who is easily fooled; a gullible person.
words.example: The gudgeon believed almost everything he was told, no matter how improbable.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French 'goujon', from Latin 'gobio(n-)', from 'gobius' 'goby fish', the fish being easily caught.

words.definition: Initiative, usually implying a lack of outside prompting. Enterprise. Spunk.
words.example: Although he sometimes lacked common sense, you had to admire his gumption in attempting to climb Mount Everest.
words.origin: Early 18th century (originally Scots): of unknown origin.

words.definition: To eat or drink greedily and noisily.
words.example: As the man across from her guttled his meal, Sabrina knew the blind date had been a mistake.
words.origin: 17th century: from 'gut', on the pattern of 'guzzle'.

words.definition: A dealer in men's clothing.
words.example: Five minutes after receiving the job offer, John visited his haberdasher to buy a new suit.
words.origin: Late Middle English: probably from Anglo-Norman French 'hapertas', perhaps the name of a fabric, of unknown origin.

words.definition: Lecture (someone) at length in an aggressive and critical manner.
words.example: Karen was left somewhat flushed in the face after haranguing the office junior for repeatedly using her designated parking space.
words.origin: Late 15th century: from Old French 'arenge', from medieval Latin 'harenga', perhaps of Germanic origin.

words.definition: A person or thing that announces or signals the approach of another; something that foreshadows a future event.
words.example: A breeze cut across the landscape, a harbinger of the approaching change in seasons.
words.origin: Middle English (denoting a person who provided lodging): from Old French 'herbergere', from 'herbergier' 'provide lodging'.

words.definition: To clear the throat in a pompous way.
words.example: The councilman harrumphed whenever he disagreed with his fellow board members' comments.
words.origin: Early 20th century: imitative of the sound of clearing the throat.

words.definition: Harass; worry to the point of distraction; assail with bothersome thoughts or acts.
words.example: After discovering Beth's secret interest in another man, Eric was harried by self-doubt until he realized her actions had nothing to do with him and everything to do with her inability to commit.
words.origin: Old English 'hergian', of Germanic origin; related to Dutch 'heer' and German 'Heer' 'army'.

words.definition: Arrogantly superior and disdainful.
words.example: As he entered the club, Tyler shot those behind the velvet ropes a haughty look and tipped the brim on his fedora.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: extended form of archaic 'haught' 'high in one's own estimation', from Old French 'haut' 'high', from Latin 'altus'.

words.definition: Mental dullness or lethargy.
words.example: Free-roaming domestic cats compensate for their depredations on wildlife --and the intellectual hebetude of their owners-- by dying much sooner than indoor cats.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from late Latin 'hebetudo', from Latin 'hebes' 'blunt'.

words.definition: In a disordered manner; helter-skelter.
words.example: When the school bell rang, a throng of shrieking children ran higgledy-piggledy down the hill to the Piggly-Wiggly for snacks and sodas.
words.origin: Late 16th century: rhyming jingle, probably with reference to the irregular herding of pigs.

words.definition: To walk with a limp; hobble.
words.example: He hirpled down the sidewalk, wincing in pain from the fall on the ice.
words.origin: Late Middle English (Scots and northern English): of unknown origin, probably imitative.

words.definition: Hairy.
words.example: The cliche of manliness, he lumbered into the room clutching a bottle of beer, his hirsute chest peeking out from a flannel shirt that was only partially buttoned.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin 'hirsutus'.

words.definition: Overly theatrical or melodramatic in character or style.
words.example: He was given to being histrionic whenever an inconvenience surfaced.
words.origin: Late 17th century: from late Latin 'histrionicus', from Latin 'histrio(n-)' 'actor'.

words.definition: To ingratiate or seek to ingratiate oneself so as to cheat or deceive.
words.example: He honeyfuggled the judges, lavishing compliments until he walked away with the competition.
words.origin: 19th-century US: of uncertain origin, perhaps related to dialect 'coney-fugle' 'to dupe by flattery'.

words.definition: An informal gathering with folk music and sometimes dancing.
words.example: When we went back to the family farm and my uncle picked up the guitar, our holiday gatherings turned into lively hootenannies.
words.origin: 1920s US: originally meaning a gadget or thingamajig; of unknown origin.

words.definition: To deceive.
words.example: Bernie Madoff's brilliant Ponzi scheme secured his spot in the hornswogglers' hall of fame.
words.origin: Early 19th century US: of unknown fanciful origin.

words.definition: A loose belted overgown of the 14th and 15th centuries usually with long wide sleeves, dagged edges, a fur lining, and full-length skirt often with slits in it.
words.example: She toiled over her homemade houppelande for months, proudly debuting it at the Renaissance faire to the admiration of her friends.
words.origin: From Old French 'houppelande', of uncertain origin.

words.definition: A loud noise or disturbance; uproar; commotion.
words.example: The kids living behind me were raising such a hullabaloo that at 3 a.m. I finally called the cops.
words.origin: Mid 18th century: perhaps a rhyming reduplication of 'hullo' or related to Scots 'baloo' 'lullaby'.

words.definition: Rubbish; nonsense; a fraud or impostor.
words.example: He heard the noise much louder on the floors below; then coming up the stairs; then coming straight toward his door. "It's humbug still!" said Scrooge. "I won't believe it." (Charles Dickens, "A Christmas Carol")
words.origin: Mid 18th century: of unknown origin.

words.definition: A remarkable or extraordinary person, place, action or thing.
words.example: I've been in big storms before, but that Sandy was a real humdinger.
words.origin: Early 20th century US: of uncertain origin, perhaps from 'hum' and 'ding'.

words.definition: An imaginary illness or pain.
words.example: After searching the internet for symptoms, he walked away with a dozen more humdudgeons.
words.origin: 18th century: probably a fanciful formation from 'humbug' and 'dudgeon'.

words.definition: To lounge in bed long after it's time to get up.
words.example: Even though he set four alarms, John still hurkle-durkled until his carpool driver knocked on his door.
words.origin: 18th-century Scots dialect, of obscure imitative origin.

words.definition: Noisy tumult and confusion.
words.example: When shall we three meet again? In thunder, lightning, or in rain? When the hurly-burly's done. When the battle's lost and won... (Macbeth, Act I, Scene I)
words.origin: Early 16th century: probably based on the verb 'hurl', with reduplication.

words.definition: An obvious and deliberate exaggeration in speech or writing, not intended to be taken literally.
words.example: In an election year, campaigners' claims that sound like hyperbole are often meant to be accepted as fact.
words.origin: Late Middle English: via Latin from Greek 'huperbolē' 'excess', from 'huper' 'above' and 'ballein' 'to throw'.

words.definition: A person who criticizes or opposes beliefs and practices that are widely accepted.
words.example: Ever the iconoclast, Everett refused to tip, never voted, and thought "The Wizard of Oz" was horrible tripe.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from medieval Latin 'iconoclastes', from ecclesiastical Greek 'eikonoklastēs', from 'eikōn' 'image' and 'klan' 'to break'.

words.definition: Not honorable in character or purpose.
words.example: He felt the ignoble sting of jealousy when he saw the two together.
words.origin: Late 15th century: from French, or from Latin 'ignobilis', from 'in-' 'not' and 'nobilis' 'noble'.

words.definition: Shameful or disgraceful.
words.example: The Rams were stunned that they went down to such an ignominious defeat.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from French 'ignominieux' or Latin 'ignominiosus', from 'ignominia' 'disgrace'.

words.definition: Alluring; attractive; enticing.
words.example: She sat at the end of the bar, illecebrous even in the smoky, neon haze.
words.origin: From Latin 'illecebrosus', from 'illecebra' 'allurement', from 'illicere' 'to entice'.

words.definition: A complicated disagreement; a confused or chaotic situation. From the Italian "imbrogliare," meaning to tangle.
words.example: Sudanese diplomats seem to think that if they look the other way long enough, their destructive domestic imbroglio will simply disappear.
words.origin: Mid 18th century: Italian, from 'imbrogliare' 'to confuse, tangle', related to 'embroil'.

words.definition: Barely felt or discernible; intangible but nonetheless perceptible.
words.example: The thick fog in the dark woods brought with it an impalpable dread.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from medieval Latin 'impalpabilis', from 'in-' 'not' and late Latin 'palpabilis' 'palpable'.

words.definition: Poor; penniless.
words.example: I would love to donate to charity, but I regret to say that at the moment I am impecunious.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from 'in-' 'not' and obsolete 'pecunious' 'having money', from Latin 'pecunia' 'money'.

words.definition: Overbearing; arrogant; dictatorial.
words.example: In public my boss was Mr. Congeniality; in the office he was an imperious jerk, making us all feel small and incompetent.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin 'imperiosus', from 'imperium' 'command, authority, empire'.

words.definition: Marked by extreme calm; serene
words.example: Tom managed to remain imperturbable even though the conversation was about politics!
words.origin: Late Middle English: from late Latin 'imperturbabilis', from 'in-' 'not' and 'perturbare' 'disturb greatly'.

words.definition: Not showing due respect for another person; impertinent.
words.example: He walked into the CEO's office in an impudent manner, putting his feet up on the desk and rolling his eyes throughout the conversation.
words.origin: Late Middle English (originally meaning 'immodest'): from Latin 'impudent-', from 'in-' 'not' and 'pudent-' 'being ashamed', from 'pudere' 'be ashamed'.

words.definition: Unable to take effective action; powerless.
words.example: Tom wanted to take action, but as he was the only person on the city council who perceived the danger, he was impuissant to make any change.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from French, from Latin in- 'not' + posse 'be able'.

words.definition: Just begun and so not fully formed or developed; rudimentary.
words.example: The inchoate organization was a mess of squabbling and confusion as the members learned how to work with each other.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin inchoatus, past participle of inchoare 'begin'.

words.definition: Not composed of matter; having no material existence
words.example: He woke up in the middle of the night to see the incorporeal presence of his long-deceased grandfather standing in the hallway.
words.origin: Late 15th century: from Latin incorporeus 'without a body' + -al.

words.definition: Unchangeable; beyond correction; impervious to reform.
words.example: We tried for years to stop Roger from bothering women in bars, but he was an incorrigible jerk.
words.origin: Middle English: from Latin incorrigibilis, from in- 'not' + corrigere 'to correct'.

words.definition: Instill (an attitude, idea, or habit) by persistent instruction.
words.example: Liturgy inculcates a spirit of worship and devotion in parishioners.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin inculcat- 'pressed in', from inculcare, from in- 'into' + calcare 'to tread'.

words.definition: (1) Lacking propriety in manners and conduct; (2) Not in accord with accepted standards of appropriate behavior polite society
words.example: Noticing the looks he was getting, Edmund feared that he'd done something seriously indecorous without realizing it.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin indecorus, from in- 'not' + decorus 'seemly'.

words.definition: Tireless; endlessly persistent.
words.example: The English privateer Francis Drake was indefatigable in his pursuit of Spanish gold.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from obsolete French, or from Latin indefatigabilis, from in- 'not' + de- 'away, completely' + fatigare 'wear out'.

words.definition: Not able to be forgotten or removed.
words.example: The summer I worked at a homeless shelter made an indelible impression on me and changed the course of my life.
words.origin: Late 15th century: from Latin indelebilis, from in- 'not' + delebilis 'able to be deleted'.

words.definition: Beyond doubt. Undeniable.
words.example: It is an indubitable truth that Wayne State is one of the nation's finest universities.
words.origin: Late 15th century: from medieval Latin indubitabilis, from in- 'not' + dubitabilis 'that may be doubted'.

words.definition: Too great, powerful or beautiful to be described or expressed.
words.example: We sat atop the hill late into the night, gazing at the stars in their ineffable glory.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French, or from Latin ineffabilis, from in- 'not' + effabilis 'able to be expressed'.

words.definition: Unavoidable; inevitable; inescapable.
words.example: Hitler's plans for domination rested on his conviction of the ineluctable superiority of the German people.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin ineluctabilis, from in- 'not' + eluctari 'struggle out'.

words.definition: Having harmful effects; hostile.
words.example: Smoking is almost universally regarded as inimical to good health.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from late Latin inimicalis, from Latin inimicus 'enemy'.

words.definition: Matchless. One of a kind.
words.example: This is an inimitable meal, a princely assembly of delicious tastes and intriguing textures.
words.origin: Late 15th century: from Latin inimitabilis, from in- 'not' + imitabilis 'able to be imitated'.

words.definition: Harmless.
words.example: The seemingly innocuous software code fix caused the entire East Coast electrical grid to fail.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin innocuus, from in- 'not' + nocuus 'injurious'.

words.definition: Lacking sense or understanding.
words.example: His blog was thousands of words of insensate gibberish.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from ecclesiastical Latin insensatus, from in- 'not' + sensatus 'having senses'.

words.definition: Producing serious harm in a stealthy, often gradual, manner. Treacherous. (Wiktionary)
words.example: It was a disease so insidious that most people were in the final stages before they even knew they had it.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin insidiosus 'cunning', from insidiae 'an ambush'.

words.definition: Dull; lacking qualities that excite, interest or stimulate.
words.example: We were expecting an acerbic and lively wit, so his insipid monologue was a great disappointment.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from French insipide or late Latin insipidus, from in- 'not' + sapidus 'savoury'.

words.definition: Insulting, caustic, acerbic and disrespectful.
words.example: He found himself strangely attracted to her pert demeanor and insolent wit.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin insolent- 'immoderate, unaccustomed, arrogant', from in- 'not' + solent- 'being accustomed'.

words.definition: The quality of being carefree; a lack of concern.
words.example: We spent our two weeks at the beach in blissful insouciance, sleeping late and basking in the sun.
words.origin: Mid 18th century: French, from insouciant, from in- 'not' + souciant 'caring'.

words.definition: Impossible to overcome.
words.example: He never considered an obstacle insuperable; if a mountain were in his path, he'd simply learn to climb.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin insuperabilis, from in- 'not' + superabilis 'able to be overcome'.

words.definition: The intellectual elite.
words.example: Contrary to ages past, the intelligentsia no longer rule the United States.
words.origin: Early 20th century: from Russian intelligentsiya, from Polish inteligencja, from Latin intelligentia 'intelligence'.

words.definition: Forbid; prohibit; exclude.
words.example: "Our company's mission is to interdict insurgent activity and also interdict insurgent movement." (Lt. Christian Garrels, serving on the Afghanistan-Pakistan border, in an NPR interview.)
words.origin: Middle English enterditen, from Old French entredit, from Latin interdictum, from interdicere 'interpose, forbid by decree'.

words.definition: Someone who takes part in a conversation or dialogue. In music, the emcee of a minstrel show.
words.example: Explanations that continually remind one's interlocutor of one's ignorance are a great damper upon the easy flow of talk. (Wiktionary)
words.origin: Early 16th century: from Latin interlocut- 'interrupted (by speech)', from interloqui, from inter- 'between' + loqui 'speak'.

words.definition: Endless (often used hyperbolically).
words.example: The meeting was an interminable marathon of pointless PowerPoint slides and insipid anecdotes.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from late Latin interminabilis, from in- 'not' + terminare 'terminate'.

words.definition: Destructive to both sides in a conflict.
words.example: The internecine strife left heavy casualties throughout the civil war-torn region.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin internecinus, from internecare 'slaughter, exterminate', from inter- 'among' + necare 'to kill'.

words.definition: Literally the interval between the end of a sovereign's reign and the accession of a successor. Also has come to mean any interruption in leadership or, more rarely, any break in continuity.
words.example: England experienced an interregnum in 1649-1660, when no king ruled and Oliver Cromwell was Lord Protector. ... OR ... The school had to close during the interregnum caused by the headmaster's unexpected dismissal.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Latin, from inter- 'between' + regnum 'reign'.

words.definition: Unwilling to compromise; incapable of being swayed or diverted; not susceptible to persuasion.
words.example: Despite his personal tragedy, Nick remained intransigent on proposals for handgun control.
words.origin: Late 19th century: from French intransigeant, from Spanish los intransigentes, from in- 'not' + transigente 'compromising'.

words.definition: Accustom (someone) to something, especially something unpleasant.
words.example: He used to blush at the most PG-rated obscenity, but a decade of cable TV had inured him to profanity.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from an Anglo-Norman French phrase based on Old French en 'in' + uvre 'work'.

words.definition: Insulting, abusive or highly critical language
words.example: While he appeared shy, on the Internet he was a bully who could unleash invective like no one else.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French invectif, from late Latin invectivus 'attacking', from invehere 'carry in'.

words.definition: Tending to cause envy, discontent or animosity.
words.example: In the 20th century, the art of selling goods to consumers through invidious comparisons was honed to a keen edge.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin invidiosus, from invidia 'envy'.

words.definition: Irritable; easily provoked; quick-tempered.
words.example: "I have never met a poet worth a damn that was not irascible." (Ezra Pound, American expatriate poet, 1885-1972)
words.origin: Late Middle English: from late Latin irascibilis, from Latin irasci 'grow angry', from ira 'anger'.

words.definition: An impudent or conceited fellow.
words.example: Mr. Hughes had his neighbor's son pegged as a disrespectful jackanapes and was therefore reluctant to hire him to shovel the driveway.
words.origin: Late Middle English: probably from Jack of Naples, perhaps applied to a tame ape or monkey.

words.definition: An automobile that is in a decrepit state.
words.example: He drove up in the ugliest old green jalopy I have ever seen.
words.origin: 1920s (originally US): of unknown origin.

words.definition: A practical joke.
words.example: He was uptight and couldn't handle his friends' good-natured japes.
words.origin: Late Middle English: perhaps from Old French japer 'to yelp' or gaber 'to mock'.

words.definition: To jumble or confuse.
words.example: He threw the book down, jargogled by the nonsense words and endless sentences.
words.origin: Late 17th century: of uncertain origin, possibly related to 'jargon'.

words.definition: A short excursion or journey for pleasure.
words.example: The long weekend allowed just enough time for a jaunt through the Upper Peninsula.
words.origin: Late 16th century: of unknown origin.

words.definition: Childish; unsophisticated; naive; dull or uninteresting; lacking nutritive value.
words.example: Having been away for years, I was surprised by my relatives' jejune behavior around the Thanksgiving table.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin jejunus 'fasting, barren'.

words.definition: Of or pertaining to a breakfast taken early in the morning or immediately on getting up.
words.example: I took a post-jentacular walk to aid my digestion.
words.origin: 17th century: from Latin jentaculum 'a breakfast taken immediately on getting up'.

words.definition: A long and mournful story, often prophesying doom or at least decrying the sad state of society. From the Biblical "Lamentations," attributed to the prophet Jeremiah.
words.example: If you listened seriously to Bruce's jeremiads, you'd have thought the country was headed for the dumpster.
words.origin: Late 18th century: from French jérémiade, from Jérémie 'Jeremiah', with reference to the Lamentations of Jeremiah.

words.definition: Abandon or discard (someone or something that is no longer wanted).
words.example: My first positive action after my ex moved out was to jettison the clutter that she had collected.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Anglo-Norman French getteson, from Latin jactatio(n-), from jactare 'to throw'.

words.definition: Hanky-panky. Skullduggery (see below).
words.example: We wouldn't be in this mess if the banks hadn't engaged in so much jiggery-pokery with those credit default swaps.
words.origin: Late 19th century: probably a variant of Scots jouk-pawk 'a trick', from jouk 'to dodge'.

words.definition: To spill (a liquid) by shaking or unsteady moving of the vessel; to pour out unsteadily.
words.example: He ran to the car in a rush, jirbling his coffee from his mug onto his new suit.
words.origin: 18th century: of Scots origin, of uncertain derivation.

words.definition: A hypothetical conversation that you compulsively play out in your head.
words.example: His commute involved a nerve-jangling series of jouskas as he prepared for the stressors and obstacles that awaited.
words.origin: Coined by John Koenig in The Dictionary of Obscure Sorrows.

words.definition: An informal social gathering at which coffee is served.
words.example: After the kids got on the bus, the parents headed to the porch for their morning kaffeeklatsch.
words.origin: Late 19th century: from German Kaffee 'coffee' + Klatsch 'gossip'.

words.definition: To cackle or laugh.
words.example: Dad was usually a quiet person, but reading the morning funnies would often cause him to toss back his head and keak loudly, waking us all.
words.origin: Of Scots and Northern English dialect origin, imitative of a shrill cry.

words.definition: Commotion, uproar, tumult, brouhaha.
words.example: The perennial kerfuffle over public radio seems largely a pastime of those who would profit most from listening to it.
words.origin: Early 19th century: perhaps from Scots curfuffle, from fuffle 'to disorder'.

words.definition: A roguish or mischievous act.
words.example: His presidency was founded on malice, lies and knavery.
words.origin: Late 15th century: from knave (from Old English cnafa 'boy, servant') + -ery.

words.definition: To make loud, useless noises.
words.example: I couldn't concentrate on my work with the kids kyoodling in the next room.
words.origin: Early 20th century (originally US dialect): imitative of a dog's yelping.

words.definition: Tearful; deeply gloomy; mournful.
words.example: After the death of his poodle, Roger was lachrymose for weeks.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin lacrimosus, from lacrima 'tear'.

words.definition: A gap or absent part, as in a manuscript or logical argument; a hiatus. From the Latin "lacuna," for ditch, hole or gap. The preferred plural is "lacunae."
words.example: The lacuna in my adversary's meticulously sequential argument was so glaring that I was reluctant to point it out.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin lacuna 'pool', from lacus 'lake, pool'.

words.definition: Moving slowly; sluggish; snail-paced; dilatory.
words.example: Driven to dyspepsia by the restaurant's laggard service, Jeremy ostentatiously threw his napkin on the table and left.
words.origin: Late 17th century: from archaic lag 'hindmost person' + -ard.

words.definition: Something thrown in for free. A bonus.
words.example: If the waiter in the restaurant stumbles and spills a gill of coffee down the back of your neck, he says 'For lagniappe, sah,' and gets you another cup without extra charge. (Mark Twain in "Life on the Mississippi")
words.origin: Mid 19th century (originally Louisiana): from Louisiana French, from American Spanish la ñapa, from Quechua yapa 'addition'.

words.definition: Someone who is particularly good or successful at what they do.
words.example: Throughout school and into his career, he was a lallycodler who shot straight up the ladder of success.
words.origin: Of obscure origin, recorded as a dialectal or whimsical term.

words.definition: To beat or reprimand severely.
words.example: The Rams lambasted the 49ers today in a 74 to 0 rout.
words.origin: Late 16th century: probably from lam 'to beat' + baste 'to beat'.

words.definition: A passionate expression of grief or sorrow.
words.example: Tears fell on the keyboard as he penned a lament to the canceled television show.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin lamentum 'a wailing', via Old French lamenter or Latin lamentari.

words.definition: Displaying or having a disinclination for physical exertion or effort; slow and relaxed.
words.example: By the end of August, John was a languid lump on the couch, watching 12 hours of "The Simpsons."
words.origin: Late 16th century: from French languide or Latin languidus, from languere 'be faint or ill'.

words.definition: Listless indolence or inertia.
words.example: He knew he should mow the lawn, but the languor of the humid day got the best of him and kept him on the hammock.
words.origin: Middle English: via Old French from Latin languor, from languere 'be faint or ill'.

words.definition: Trickery, often in the guise of magic.
words.example: In listening to politicians or prestidigitators, one should consider that their convoluted word choices are often just legerdemain to manipulate their audiences.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from French léger de main 'dexterous', literally 'light of hand'.

words.definition: The inability to remember a particular word or name.
words.example: Although he had worked in the same office for nearly a decade, he was struck with a case of lethologica every time he had to greet that particular coworker.
words.origin: Early 20th century: from Greek lēthē 'forgetfulness' + logos 'word'.

words.definition: Something that uses up money.
words.example: The car was a lickpenny, in constant need of repair.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from 'lick' + 'penny', denoting something that consumes money.

words.definition: A fawning subordinate; toady.
words.example: He feigned objectivity but was little more than a lickspittle for the CEO.
words.origin: Late 19th century: from 'lick' + 'spittle', literally one who licks up spittle.

words.definition: Very small. From Lilliput, a country of teeny-weeny people in Jonathan Swift's "Gulliver's Travels."
words.example: He looked absolutely lilliputian when standing next to the basketball players.
words.origin: Early 18th century: from Lilliput, the imaginary island of tiny people in Jonathan Swift's 'Gulliver's Travels' (1726).

words.definition: Thin, supple and graceful.
words.example: She was enraptured with ballet from the moment she saw the lissome dancers glide across the stage.
words.origin: Late 18th century: variant of 'lithesome'.

words.definition: Relating to or situated on the shore of the sea or a lake.
words.example: We drove along Michigan's west coast, passing a variety of littoral towns and villages.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin litoralis, from litus, litor- 'shore'.

words.definition: Not able to think or move normally because of being tired, sick, etc.
words.example: A weekend full of chores, housework and family gatherings left Kyle a bit loggy on Monday morning.
words.origin: Early 19th century: perhaps related to 'log', suggesting heaviness or inertness.

words.definition: Excessive and often incoherent talkativeness or wordiness.
words.example: Internet blogs and podcasts foster logorrhea from effusive writers and speakers, who lay waste to years of schooling that encouraged brevity.
words.origin: Early 20th century: from Greek logos 'word' + rhoia 'flow'.

words.definition: Sit, lie or stand in a lazy, relaxed way.
words.example: The clerks lolled around the counter, giving little attention to the customers waiting for assistance.
words.origin: Late Middle English: probably imitative of dangling or hanging loosely.

words.definition: Dawdle; waste time.
words.example: If our legislators hadn't spent so much time insulting one another and just lollygagging around, maybe they'd have accomplished something.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: of uncertain origin, US dialect.

words.definition: Adj. Talkative, especially when excessively so.
words.example: The more loquacious Thenardier was, the more dumb was Jean Valjean. (Victor Hugo, in "Les Miserables")
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin loquax, loquac- (from loqui 'talk') + -ious.

words.definition: 1. Arousing sexual desire; lecherous; lascivious.
words.example: Brad swears that Katie gave him a radiantly lubricious wink, but I think she just had something in her eye.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Latin lubricus 'slippery' + -ious.

words.definition: Money, especially when regarded as sordid or distasteful or gained in a dishonorable way.
words.example: He didn't care about his clients, only the filthy lucre that helped pay for his beach house.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin lucrum 'gain'.

words.definition: Clear in thought or expression.
words.example: The interviewee was luculent and personable.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin luculentus, from lux, luc- 'light'.

words.definition: Ridiculous; worthy of mockery.
words.example: The outfit he wore to their first date was so ludibrious that she couldn't hold back her laughter.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin ludibrium 'mockery', from ludere 'to play'.

words.definition: Showing spontaneous and undirected playfulness.
words.example: Grandpa may have been getting old, but his ludic personality still delighted the kids.
words.origin: 1940s: from French ludique, from Latin ludere 'to play'.

words.definition: Extremely mournful or gloomy.
words.example: His lugubrious moods were caused by the death of his grandfather. ... OR ... "Presently a dog set up a long, lugubrious howl just outside..." (Mark Twain, "Tom Sawyer," 1876)
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin lugubris (from lugere 'mourn') + -ous.

words.definition: A clumsy, stupid person.
words.example: The big lummox barged into the museum, spilling his drink, bumping into the crowds and nearly knocking a priceless sculpture to the ground.
words.origin: Early 19th century: of uncertain origin, perhaps from English dialect.

words.definition: Plot or scheme.
words.example: His clever machinations helped him ascend to the highest levels of power.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French, from Latin machinari 'contrive', from machina 'machine'.

words.definition: Of or related to cooking.
words.example: A decade earlier, he could only make ramen noodles; now, his magiric skills had everyone in the neighborhood hoping for an invite to his dinner parties.
words.origin: From Greek mageiros 'cook'.

words.definition: Very generous or forgiving, esp. toward a rival or someone less powerful than oneself.
words.example: Jake had a right to be bitter following the election, but he was magnanimous to the end, buying a round of drinks for the reporters who'd sorted through his dirty laundry in the press.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Latin magnanimus (from magnus 'great' + animus 'soul') + -ous.

words.definition: Clumsy, inept, awkward, tactless.
words.example: Nick was so maladroit in handling the liquor, conversation and priceless china figurines at his mother-in-law's house that it's no wonder he never got invited back.
words.origin: Late 17th century: from French, from mal 'badly' + adroit 'dexterous'.

words.definition: Dismal.
words.example: He stepped outside into the malagrugrous weather, pulling his collar up to shield himself from the rain and cold.
words.origin: 19th century: of uncertain origin, perhaps Scots dialect.

words.definition: Boldly disrespectful to a person of higher standing.
words.example: He had skill, but his malapert behavior put him at odds with executives and prohibited any chance of promotion.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from mal- 'wrongly' + Old French apert 'open, frank'.

words.definition: The substitution of an incorrect word for one with a similar sound, frequently to comic effect. From the misspeaking character of Mrs. Malaprop in Sheridan's play "The Rivals" (1775).
words.example: (Example) Patience is a virgin.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from Mrs. Malaprop, a character in Sheridan's play 'The Rivals' (1775), from French mal à propos 'inappropriate'.

words.definition: Nonsense; hogwash; rubbish; speech designed to mislead.
words.example: Poor Lewis! He still believes that malarkey about President Obama's having been born in Kenya.
words.origin: 1920s: of unknown origin, originally US slang.

words.definition: A magical word or phrase uttered with the intention of bringing about evil or destruction; a curse.
words.example: Perhaps spurred on by reading too many Harry Potter books, the young boy grumbled a malediction at his siblings as he trudged down the hallway.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin maledictio(n-), from maledicere 'speak evil of'.

words.definition: Foul-smelling, odorous.
words.example: The restaurant was set at the end of the street, likely to shield the community from the maleolent fog that wafted from its kitchen.
words.origin: From Latin male 'badly' + olere 'to smell'.

words.definition: Wrongdoing, misconduct or misbehavior, especially by a public official.
words.example: The governor's story was a tawdry one of money-grubbing, cronyism and general malfeasance.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Anglo-Norman French malfaisance, from mal- 'badly' + Old French faisance 'activity'.

words.definition: One who frustrates or ruins a plan or undertaking by meddling.
words.example: Christmas dinner was all planned out until my mother-in-law, ever the marplot, decided to get involved.
words.origin: Early 18th century: from a character named Marplot in Susanna Centlivre's play 'The Busie Body' (1709).

words.definition: A strict disciplinarian; someone who insists on absolute adherence to rules. From the 17th-century French army officer Jean Martinet.
words.example: As a manager Geoffrey was such a martinet that staff meetings were mostly just his ranting about our imaginary foibles.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Jean Martinet, a 17th-century French army officer who devised a strict drill system.

words.definition: Excessively sentimental, sappy, hopelessly trite.
words.example: To her surprise, Alice found Brian's vows of love embarrassingly mawkish and cloying. Words he probably thought of as lyrical just made her feel sticky, as though she were being painted with molasses.
words.origin: Late 17th century: from obsolete 'mawk' 'maggot', from Old Norse mathkr.

words.definition: Perhaps; possibly. Usually dismissed as archaic, but what the heck.
words.example: Mayhap the crocuses will show their purple heads before Valentine's Day this year.
words.origin: Early 16th century: from the phrase 'it may hap'.

words.definition: Careful and calculated.
words.example: After considering the import of the question, the witness gave a measured response.
words.origin: Middle English: from 'measure', from Latin mensura 'measurement'.

words.definition: A profound presentation of depression and complete loss of pleasure in all or almost everything.
words.example: I have suffered Melancholia since the unfortunate and untimely demise of my first wife.
words.origin: Late Middle English: via Latin from Greek melankholia, from melas 'black' + khole 'bile'.

words.definition: A mixture of different things.
words.example: Her painting was a melange of colors and shapes that dazzled the eyes.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from French, from mêler 'to mix'.

words.definition: Smooth and flowing with sweetness.
words.example: The flute trio in "L'Enfance du Christ" is so mellifluous that it almost makes me weep every time I hear it.
words.origin: Late 15th century: from Latin mellifluus (from mel 'honey' + fluere 'to flow') + -ous.

words.definition: Untruthful; dishonest.
words.example: His mendacious claim that he had a beautiful sister led to one of my most memorable surprises.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin mendax, mendac- 'lying' + -ious.

words.definition: Pestilential, poisonous, foul-smelling, putrid, offensive.
words.example: After grading his class' term papers, Edmund felt that he had confronted and overcome something so vile and mephitic that only bourbon could reward the achievement and erase the memory.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from late Latin mephiticus, from mephitis 'noxious exhalation'.

words.definition: Fickle; erratic.
words.example: She said she needed a break from trying to anticipate my mercurial moods. I haven't seen her in five years.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin mercurialis 'relating to the god Mercury', also to the planet.

words.definition: Falsely attractive; tastelessly garish; tawdry.
words.example: (Gatsby) was a son of God ... and he must be about His Father's business, the service of a vast, vulgar and meretricious beauty. (F. Scott Fitzgerald, 1896-1940)
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin meretricius (from meretrix 'prostitute') + -ous.

words.definition: Deserving of reward or praise.
words.example: Despite his gruff exterior, Phil had been recognized several times for his meritorious work with the less fortunate.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin meritorius 'earning money' (from mereri 'earn, deserve') + -ous.

words.definition: It seems to me.
words.example: My parents' generation, methinks, largely failed to see the value in life without money.
words.origin: Old English mē thyncth 'it seems to me', from thyncan 'to seem'.

words.definition: A highly unpleasant or unhealthy smell or vapor.
words.example: A miasma of nicotine hung over the casino as the patrons' health disappeared as quickly as their wealth.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: modern Latin, from Greek miasma 'defilement', from miainein 'pollute'.

words.definition: Expressing or conveying a threat.
words.example: He punctuated his ultimatum by sticking a minatory finger into the air.
words.origin: Late 15th century: from late Latin minatorius, from minari 'to threaten'.

words.definition: Merriment; amusement accompanied by laughter.
words.example: Let us have wine and women, mirth and laughter / Sermons and soda water the day after. (Lord Byron, English poet and satirist, 1788-1824)
words.origin: Old English myrgth, from myrige 'pleasing'.

words.definition: A person who does not like other people.
words.example: His father was a bitter old misanthrope who once got into a fight with Goofy during a trip to Disney World.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Greek misanthrōpos, from misein 'to hate' + anthrōpos 'man'.

words.definition: Hatred, mistrust or rank objectification of women.
words.example: Alex managed to hide his derisive misogyny behind a veneer of jovial good manners just long enough to interview the Dallas Cowboy cheerleaders.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Greek misos 'hatred' + gunē 'woman'.

words.definition: The shadow cast by trees.
words.example: We enjoyed our ice cream while sitting in the cool of the mogshade.
words.origin: Old English: a dialectal compound, perhaps from 'mock' or 'mug' + 'shade'.

words.definition: Pacify, appease, soothe in temper.
words.example: Sheldon tried everything he knew --flowers, poetry, humor, software, pleading and self-degradation-- but Eva, radiant and giddy with fury, could not be mollified.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from French mollifier, from Latin mollificare, from mollis 'soft'.

words.definition: Treat someone very indulgently or protectively.
words.example: Henry might be more willing to step into the world if his parents didn't mollycoddle him so much, still making his lunch and washing his clothes at the age of 18.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from 'molly' (a milksop) + 'coddle'.

words.definition: A word or phrase that results from a mishearing especially of something recited or sung.
words.example: "Excuse me while I kiss this guy," a misunderstanding of Hendrix's lyrics, "excuse me while I kiss the sky," remains one of the most widely reported mondegreens of all time.
words.origin: 1950s: from a mishearing of the Scottish ballad line 'laid him on the green' as 'Lady Mondegreen', coined by Sylvia Wright.

words.definition: Marvelous, extraordinary.
words.example: The audience gave a rousing standing ovation to the cast after the monsterful performance.
words.origin: Early 19th century: from 'monster' in the sense 'huge, marvelous' + -ful.

words.definition: Open to debate (adj.). Having no legal significance (n.).
words.example: The judge has found you guilty, so your claim of self-defense is moot now.
words.origin: Old English mot 'assembly', from gemot; the adjectival sense from the practice of debating hypothetical cases in moot courts.

words.definition: (Especially of humor) having or showing a sharp or critical quality; biting.
words.example: He fell in with a group of writers known for their high-brow interests and mordant wit.
words.origin: Late 15th century: from French, present participle of mordre 'to bite', from Latin mordere.

words.definition: Mark with spots or smears of color.
words.example: She walked in to find her toddler on the floor, the formerly white wall mottled with orange and yellow paint.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: probably a back-formation from 'motley'.

words.definition: A charlatan, quack or con artist; one who sells phony medicines from a podium or perch.
words.example: Jack presented himself as an expert on journalism, but many suspected he actually was a mountebank of the first water.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Italian montambanco, from the imperative phrase monta in banco! 'climb on the bench!'.

words.definition: Force of character, determination or nerve.
words.example: Her resume wasn't the most accomplished, but she showed enough moxie during the interview process to land the job.
words.origin: 1930s: from Moxie, a US proprietary name for a soft drink.

words.definition: Bring into a disordered or confusing state.
words.example: The speaker got the facts right, but muddled the message with his delivery.
words.origin: Late Middle English: perhaps from Middle Dutch moddelen 'make muddy', from modde 'mud'.

words.definition: A person who remains aloof or independent, especially from party politics.
words.example: Ever the mugwump, John refused to take a side in the partisan bickering that divided his office.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from Algonquian mugquomp 'great chief', applied to US Republicans who refused to support their party's candidate in 1884.

words.definition: Rubbish, refuse, dirt.
words.example: As he dug through the pile of mullock, Todd cursed himself for once again throwing his keys away.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from 'mull' 'dust, rubbish', of Scandinavian origin; later used in Australian mining for waste rock.

words.definition: Sullen; sulky.
words.example: The cheery kid turned into a mumpish adolescent about the time he hit middle school.
words.origin: Early 18th century: from mump 'to sulk' (of unknown origin) + -ish.

words.definition: A traditional custom or notion adhered to although shown to be unreasonable.
words.example: He grumbled as he put on his suit and tie, a mumpsimus the company required even though he never met with any clients face to face.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: erroneous Latin mumpsimus, repeated by an illiterate priest for the correct sumpsimus in the Mass.

words.definition: Very generous; extremely liberal in giving.
words.example: A wall in our new arts center honors the munificent family for whom the building is named.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Latin munificus 'bountiful', from munus 'gift', on the pattern of magnificent.

words.definition: (adj.)A large, indefinite number; made up of many diverse elements. ... "A myriad of" is gaining acceptance only because those who don't know any better brazenly continue to use it.
words.example: It was a clear October night, with no moon and a myriad stars blazing overhead.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: via late Latin from Greek murias, muriad-, from murioi 'ten thousand'.

words.definition: A subordinate who follows orders without question. From the Myrmidons, who followed Achilles to Troy.
words.example: No matter how many times I tried to convince him that his boss was a criminal, he was such a myrmidon that my arguments fell on deaf ears.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin Myrmidones (plural), from Greek Murmidones, a warlike people of Thessaly who followed Achilles to Troy.

words.definition: Lowest point.
words.example: Bill Clinton's nadir was his impeachment by the House of Representatives.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Arabic naẓīr (as-samt) 'opposite (the zenith)'.

words.definition: Coming or having recently come into existence.
words.example: His nascent film career is off to a promising start; he received an Oscar nomination for his debut project.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin nascent-, nasci 'be born'.

words.definition: To talk aimlessly, often at length.
words.example: You can tell our staff meetings are winding down when everyone starts nattering about their kids.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: variant of dialect gnatter 'grumble, chatter', of imitative origin.

words.definition: A good-for-nothing, ineffectual person.
words.example: Your ne'er-do-well brother just got fired from his job cleaning toilets at the community center.
words.origin: Early 18th century: from the phrase ne'er do well 'never do well', originally Scots.

words.definition: A timid, meek, or ineffectual person.
words.example: He was a nebbish who never spoke up and never moved out of his parents' guest room.
words.origin: 1890s: from Yiddish nebekh 'poor thing', of Slavic origin.

words.definition: A person who is new to a subject, skill or belief.
words.example: Ever the neophyte, he displayed passion that compensated for his mistakes.
words.origin: Late Middle English: via ecclesiastical Latin from Greek neophutos 'newly planted', from neos 'new' + phuton 'plant'.

words.definition: Irritate or annoy (someone).
words.example: The constant buzzing from the air conditioner nettled Isaac as he tried to write the letter.
words.origin: Old English netele, of Germanic origin; the verb sense 'irritate' dates from the mid 16th century.

words.definition: Snowy or resembling snow.
words.example: There were few better childhood memories than waking up to a niveous haze outside and hearing the television down the hall announce that school was closed.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin niveus 'snowy', from nix, niv- 'snow'.

words.definition: Offensive, unwholesome; disgusting; disagreeable, foul-smelling.
words.example: The governor was a character so noisome that even his allies avoided him when they could.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from obsolete noy (shortened form of annoy) + -some.

words.definition: To bewilder or confound (v.) ... A state of confusion (n.)
words.example: If you can't awe them with acumen, then nonplus them with nonsense.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Latin non plus 'not more', the point at which one can say no more.

words.definition: Pester or nag.
words.example: His son continued to noodge him about signing up for football, but he still forgot the deadline.
words.origin: 1960s: from Yiddish nudyen 'to bore, pester', of Slavic origin.

words.definition: Of no value or importance.
words.example: He rambled on for hours, his big words masking the nugatory contribution he made to the debate.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin nugatorius, from nugari 'to trifle', from nugae 'jests'.

words.definition: Awe-inspiring; profoundly moving; evocative of transcendence. (Despite what Webster's Dictionary says, it never presumes the supernatural.)
words.example: As the full moon rose in numinous splendor over Mount Kilimanjaro, Ernie was stricken speechless with wonder and joy.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin numen, numin- 'divine will' + -ous.

words.definition: To walk about.
words.example: During our vacation, my wife and I would wake up early and obambulate around the empty beach.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin obambulat-, obambulare 'to walk about', from ob- 'towards' + ambulare 'to walk'.

words.definition: Stubbornly refusing to change one's opinion or course of action.
words.example: I encouraged him to change his spending habits, but he was obdurate and continued living well beyond his means.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin obduratus, from obdurare 'harden against', from ob- 'against' + durare 'harden'.

words.definition: To make something obscure or otherwise hard to understand.
words.example: Despite his use of abstruse language, he was not consciously trying to obfuscate his subject matter.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from late Latin obfuscat-, obfuscare 'to darken', from ob- 'over' + fuscus 'dark'.

words.definition: Indirect, slanting. Devious.
words.example: Janet choose an oblique route so as to get home from school unnoticed.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin obliquus.

words.definition: Abusive language, defamation or denunciation. Also the ill repute, deserved or not, that results from such abuse.
words.example: Any artist daring to transgress the ordinary must be willing to weather the obloquy of critics.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from late Latin obloquium 'contradiction', from Latin obloqui, from ob- 'against' + loqui 'speak'.

words.definition: Overly deferential or attentive; fawningly submissive.
words.example: If my co-workers were a little less obsequious around our boss, I might get more promotions.
words.origin: Late 15th century: from Latin obsequiosus, from obsequium 'compliance', from obsequi 'follow, comply with'.

words.definition: Unruly, noisy, clamorous.
words.example: The obstreperous children at the adjacent table gave me a headache.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin obstreperus, from ob- 'against' + strepere 'make a noise', + -ous.

words.definition: Dull. Slow on the uptake.
words.example: If you weren't so obtuse, you would understand the problem.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin obtusus, past participle of obtundere 'beat against, blunt'.

words.definition: A state of disgrace resulting from hateful conduct. Also contempt.
words.example: Long after the Civil War, the South suffered the odium of having fought to keep a system that condoned slavery.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin, literally 'hatred', from odisse 'to hate'.

words.definition: The works of a painter, composer, or author regarded collectively.
words.example: Charles Dickens' oeuvre contains some of literature's most beloved characters.
words.origin: Late 19th century: French, literally 'work', from Latin opera.

words.definition: 1. Having the properties of oil; greasy. 2. Smarmy and unctuous; falsely sincere.
words.example: After I'd seen so many horrifying images from the Gulf, the oleaginous waffling of BP officials was almost more than I could stand.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from French oléagineux, from Latin oleaginus 'of the olive tree', from olea 'olive'.

words.definition: (Of a task, duty, or responsibility) involving an amount of effort and difficulty that is oppressively burdensome.
words.example: He tried to make the updates to the house himself but found the tasks onerous and expensive.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French onereus, from Latin onerosus, from onus, oner- 'burden'.

words.definition: Showing varying colors as an opal does.
words.example: Paul looked up at the opalescent sky.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from opal + -escent, on the pattern of words such as fluorescent.

words.definition: Harsh criticism; public disgrace resulting from shameful conduct.
words.example: In a more innocent era, baseless insinuations were a way to attract opprobrium; now, in politics, they're often a way to attract support.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin, literally 'infamy', from opprobrum, from ob- 'against' + probrum 'disgraceful act'.

words.definition: A person who begins to learn or study only late in life.
words.example: Uninterested in anything intellectual for most of his life, my father turned into an opsimath after retirement, attending lectures and always carrying a book.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Greek opsimathēs, from opse 'late' + manthanein 'learn'.

words.definition: Haughty, proud.
words.example: The king walked out among the crowd, looking orgulous and regal in his fine robes.
words.origin: Middle English: from Old French orgeillos, from orgeuil 'pride'.

words.definition: (1) Pompous or bombastic. (2) Characterized by fullness, clarity and strength of sound.
words.example: The emperor Hadrian had a lifelong fondness for the most orotund and pretentious of historians.
words.origin: Late 18th century: from Latin ore rotundo 'with rounded mouth'.

words.definition: To harden like bone; to become set in one's ways. (From the Latin "os," for bone.)
words.example: Bert felt as though if he had to wait for Alice any longer he was just going to ossify right there on her doorstep.
words.origin: Late 17th century: from French ossifier, from Latin os, oss- 'bone'.

words.definition: Characterized by vulgar or pretentious display; designed to impress or attract notice.
words.example: He had an ostentatious collection of books he'd never read sitting on his shelf, giving him an unearned reputation for brilliance.
words.origin: Late 17th century: from French ostentation, from Latin ostentatio(n-), from ostentare 'display ostentatiously'.

words.definition: Serving no practical purpose or result.
words.example: He asked for suggestions but was met by the otiose ramblings of his disinterested peers.
words.origin: Late 18th century: from Latin otiosus, from otium 'leisure'.

words.definition: Domineering, imperious, condescending, assertive. Prone to take advantage of power.
words.example: The overmighty zoning officers dismissed the complaint with insulting condescension.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from over- 'excessively' + mighty.

words.definition: Arrogant; excessive.
words.example: The team's overweening confidence prevented them from acknowledging their weaknesses.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from the verb overween 'be conceited' (from over- + ween 'to think'), + -ing.

words.definition: Discussion; also idle talk; also unnecessarily time-consuming chatter. From the Portuguese "palavra," or "talk," or the Spanish "palabra," or "word."
words.example: We could have a palaver and decide which one is better.
words.origin: Early 18th century: from Portuguese palavra 'word', from Latin parabola 'comparison'.

words.definition: The use of repetition to create emphasis.
words.example: When the aspiring writer was asked why he repeated words so often in his project, he simply responded, "Palilogy, palilogy!"
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Greek palillogia, from palin 'again' + logos 'word'.

words.definition: An old vellum or parchment document from which the original words have been scraped away to permit new writing. "Many historical texts have been recovered using ultraviolet light and other technologies to read the erased writing." (Wiktionary)
words.example: Through careful examination of palimpsests, scholars have discovered ancient texts once believed lost. ... OR ... Clasp, Angel of the backward look / And folded wings of ashen gray / And voice of echoes far away, / The brazen covers of thy book; /The weird palimpsest old and vast, Wherein thou hid'st the spectral past... (John Greenleaf Whittier, "Snowbound," 1865)
words.origin: Mid 17th century: via Latin from Greek palimpsēstos, from palin 'again' + psēstos 'rubbed smooth'.

words.definition: Disguise the seriousness or gravity of (an offense).
words.example: He searched for an excuse, but there was no way to palliate what had happened.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from late Latin palliat-, palliare 'to cloak', from pallium 'cloak'.

words.definition: A stupid, uncouth person; a lout.
words.example: The big palooka stumbled into the reception hall, knocking over a glass on a nearby table and looking out of place among the city's elite.
words.origin: 1920s: of unknown origin, popularized in the US through boxing slang.

words.definition: (Of an amount) small or meager.
words.example: He worked for 12 hours on the lawn, but was paid a paltry fee for his efforts.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: probably from dialect paltry 'rubbish', from palt, pelt 'rags, trash'.

words.definition: Distinctive, often flamboyant, style or action. Verve.
words.example: His writing style could never have the panache of mine; I mean, who does that Charles Dickens think he is?
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from French panache, from Italian pennacchio, from late Latin pinnaculum, diminutive of pinna 'feather'.

words.definition: Formal, elaborate public praise; an encomium.
words.example: The senator's response was no panegyric, but it was nevertheless a serviceable endorsement of the candidate.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from French panégyrique, via Latin from Greek panēgurikos 'of public assembly', from pan 'all' + aguris 'assembly'.

words.definition: An important person -- or one who just thinks he is.
words.example: The mayor lived up to his image as a panjandrum by dismissing the resolution with no argument.
words.origin: Late 18th century: invented word in a nonsense passage by Samuel Foote to test the memory of the actor Charles Macklin.

words.definition: An impressive array. ... Oddly enough, panoply also can mean a full suit of armor, originating in the Greek "panoplia" or "all weapons."
words.example: I know spring is coming because I awoke this morning to a welcoming panoply of bird songs.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from French panoplie or modern Latin panoplia 'full armour', from Greek, from pan 'all' + hopla 'arms'.

words.definition: Someone who lives by the sea.
words.example: He escaped the noise of the city, bought a beach house on the Pacific coast and became a paralian.
words.origin: From Greek paralia 'seacoast', from para 'beside' + hals 'sea'.

words.definition: Extreme importance.
words.example: It is paramount that President Obama address this country's economic calamity.
words.origin: Early 16th century: from Anglo-Norman French paramont, from Old French par 'by' + amont 'above'.

words.definition: Personal belongings; articles or equipment used in a particular activity or by a specific profession; according to common law, a married woman's personal property excluding her dowry.
words.example: Harold realized he had finally passed the point at which all his worldly paraphernalia would fit in his car.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from medieval Latin paraphernalia (bona) '(goods) in a woman's personal possession', from late Latin parapherna, from Greek 'property apart from a dowry'.

words.definition: Figure of speech in which the latter part of a sentence or phrase is surprising or unexpected; frequently used in a humorous situation.
words.example: He was a funny guy, always equipped with a clever paraprosdokian, such as, "Some people are like Slinkies ... not really good for anything, but you can't help smiling when you see one tumble down the stairs."
words.origin: 20th century: from Greek para 'against' + prosdokia 'expectation'.

words.definition: Cover (a part of a building, especially an external brick wall) with plaster or mortar that typically bears an ornamental pattern.
words.example: As he began to parget the restaurant's walls, his vision for the establishment came to life before his eyes.
words.origin: Late Middle English: of unknown origin; perhaps related to Old French parjeter 'throw over'.

words.definition: Outcast; someone to be avoided.
words.example: Donald suspected that his irrational fear of turkeys had made him a pariah at even his family's Thanksgiving celebration.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Tamil paṟaiyar, plural of paṟaiyan, denoting a member of a low caste, literally 'hereditary drummer'.

words.definition: Dangerous; risky.
words.example: Prospects for Yazoo City grew increasingly parlous as the Mississippi's record flood rolled southward.
words.origin: Late Middle English: contraction of perilous.

words.definition: A sudden, uncontrollable outburst.
words.example: Leonard was surprised, to say the least, when his pledge of love sent Emily into paroxysms of laughter.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from French paroxysme, via medieval Latin from Greek paroxusmos, from paroxunein 'exasperate', from para 'beyond' + oxunein 'sharpen'.

words.definition: Excessively frugal; stingy; miserly.
words.example: His writing was so parsimonious with words that reading it took as much effort, and stimulated as much creative thought, as a game of tic-tac-toe.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Latin parsimonia, from parcere 'be sparing', + -ous.

words.definition: A person of obscure origin who has gained wealth, influence or celebrity.
words.example: The young parvenue walked into the glitzy Hollywood club like she'd been there all her life, but no one knew her name a year ago.
words.origin: Early 19th century: from French, feminine of parvenu, past participle of parvenir 'arrive', from Latin pervenire 'come to, reach'.

words.definition: A person's head.
words.example: After an afternoon of gardening in the hot sun, his bald pate was nearly identical to the tomatoes.
words.origin: Middle English: of unknown origin.

words.definition: Having a mocking or cynical sense of humor.
words.example: He had a pawky wit that undercut his superiors' self-importance.
words.origin: Late 16th century: of unknown origin.

words.definition: Really good.
words.example: Some days I'm peachy; other days I'm in the pits.
words.origin: Early 20th century: from peach (the fruit, suggesting something choice) plus -y.

words.definition: A trivial or petty misdeed.
words.example: The peccadillos she committed at family gatherings were used to justify her exclusion from the invitation list.
