words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Spanish pecadillo, diminutive of pecado 'sin'.

words.definition: A bit hungry. Not famished, not starving, just in the early stages of hunger.
words.example: It's been a long time since breakfast and I'm beginning to get peckish.
words.origin: Late 18th century: from peck plus -ish.

words.definition: A person who is excessively concerned with minor details and rules or with displaying academic learning.
words.example: His debut science fiction novel pulled the pedants out of the woodwork to nitpick every error and inconsistency.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from French pédant, from Italian pedante, perhaps from the Latin stem of pedagogue.

words.definition: Money, especially when gained in a dishonest or dishonorable way.
words.example: He sat on a pile of pelf that had been acquired through lies and schemes.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French pelfre 'spoils'.

words.definition: Translucently clear.
words.example: Scott gazed over the side of the boat at the school of fish swimming in the pellucid ocean water.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin pellucidus, from perlucere 'shine through'.

words.definition: Extremely careful about the way one spends even small amounts of money.
words.example: Being pennywise, he wouldn't even part with loose change to buy a soda from the vending machine.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from penny plus wise, from the proverb 'penny wise and pound foolish'.

words.definition: Next to last.
words.example: Everyone's heard of The Last Supper, but The Penultimate Supper has been largely forgotten.
words.origin: Late 17th century: from Latin paenultimus, from paene 'almost' and ultimus 'last'.

words.definition: Extremely stingy; miserly; cheap to a fault. ... Also may mean indigent.
words.example: "Gloria had tons of money, but she was so penurious that her mom had to live her last years in a crummy cold-water flat."
words.origin: Early 17th century: from medieval Latin penuriosus, from Latin penuria 'need, scarcity'.

words.definition: Walk or travel through or around a place or area, especially for pleasure and in a leisurely way.
words.example: She perambulated around the garden, taking her time to study and smell each flower.
words.origin: Early 16th century: from Latin perambulare 'walk through', from per- 'all over' and ambulare 'to walk'.

words.definition: Remain in existence throughout a substantial period of time; endure.
words.example: The myth perdured for centuries, passed down by generations of storytellers.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French perdurer, from Latin perdurare 'endure', from per- 'through' and durare 'to last'.

words.definition: A long and winding journey, a wandering.
words.example: He saw many an interesting sight and met all manner of people whilst on his peregrination.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin peregrinatio, from peregrinari 'travel abroad', from peregrinus 'foreign'.

words.definition: Insisting on immediate attention or obedience, especially in a brusquely imperious way.
words.example: If we got out of our room once after going to bed, Dad would tell us to get back in there with such peremptory force that we wouldn't even dream of getting up before the sun again.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Anglo-Norman French peremptorie, from Latin peremptorius 'deadly, decisive', from perimere 'destroy, cut off'.

words.definition: To procrastinate a long time, especially two days.
words.example: He received the order a month ago but perendinated on the work until 48 hours before the deadline.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Latin perendinare 'defer until the day after tomorrow', from perendie 'on the day after tomorrow'.

words.definition: Treachery; a deliberate breach of trust or faith.
words.example: Being dumped by Alice was bad enough, but what really galled Roger was the perfidy of his so-called friends, who knew of her dalliances and never said a word to him.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from French perfidie, from Latin perfidia, from perfidus 'treacherous', from per- 'to ill effect' and fides 'faith'.

words.definition: Carried out with a minimum of effort or reflection.
words.example: When quitting time came, I dashed down the hall, gave a perfunctory nod to my boss and headed on vacation.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from late Latin perfunctorius 'careless', from Latin perfunct- 'performed', from perfungi 'discharge, get rid of'.

words.definition: Nomadic, traveling from place to place (adj.). 2. Pedestrian, itinerant, one who walks about (n.).
words.example: The more he drank the more peripatetic he became, but his sense of direction routinely disengaged.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Greek peripatetikos 'walking up and down', from peripatein, from the Aristotelian habit of walking while teaching.

words.definition: Highly injurious or destructive.
words.example: Experts regularly debate whether pornography has a pernicious effect on society.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin perniciosus 'destructive', from pernicies 'ruin', from per- 'completely' and nex 'death'.

words.definition: Speak at length.
words.example: She stifled a yawn as her date perorated about "Star Wars" for the entirety of the meal.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin perorat- 'spoken at length', from the verb perorare, from per- 'through' and orare 'speak'.

words.definition: Banter; frivolous talk.
words.example: Emma hoped to get Lady Astor into a serious conversation, but as long as the King was around she could elicit only persiflage and harmless gossip.
words.origin: Mid 18th century: from French, from persifler 'to banter', from per- 'thoroughly' and siffler 'to whistle'.

words.definition: Very particular about details; fastidious.
words.example: Martha is persnickety with her gardens.
words.origin: Early 19th century: of unknown origin, originally Scots as pernickety.

words.definition: A person (often used to express their significance, importance, or elevated status).
words.example: He was visited by no less a personage than the president.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from medieval Latin personagium 'effigy', later influenced by French personnage.

words.definition: Discernment. Sharp and insightful intelligence.
words.example: Her perspicacity enabled her to discern the true intentions of her suitors.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Latin perspicax 'seeing clearly', from perspicere 'look through'.

words.definition: Holding firmly to an opinion or a course of action.
words.example: He remained pertinacious about the situation even as others supplied him with endless facts that contradicted his position.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin pertinax 'holding fast', from per- 'thoroughly' and tenax 'tenacious'.

words.definition: To upset, agitate or unsettle.
words.example: I hate to perturbate your plan, but you Pickwickians can't haul a codfish of that size all the way to Dingley Dell unless you use three horses.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin perturbat- 'thoroughly disturbed', from the verb perturbare, from per- 'completely' and turbare 'disturb'.

words.definition: Troublesome, bothersome, irritating, annoying.
words.example: I've made my living primarily as a science journalist, learning what evolutionary biology and ecology I know by self-education and pestiferous questioning of experts. (David Quammen)
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin pestifer 'pest-bearing', from pestis 'plague' and ferre 'to bear'.

words.definition: A pleasant smell that frequently accompanies the first rain after a long period of warm, dry weather.
words.example: He walked outside after the storm, the petrichor lifting his spirits as he hiked around the campground.
words.origin: 1960s: from Greek petra 'rock' and ichor, the fluid said to flow in the veins of the gods.

words.definition: An inferior legal practitioner, especially one who deals with petty cases or employs dubious practices.
words.example: He started with dreams of being a Supreme Court justice but in practice he was just another ambulance-chasing pettifogger.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from petty plus obsolete fogger 'underhand dealer', perhaps from the Fugger family of merchants.

words.definition: Irritable; easily annoyed; grouchy.
words.example: Nick was so petulant and bitter after his disastrous blind date that we stayed well out of his way.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from French pétulant, from Latin petulant- 'impudent', related to petere 'aim at, seek'.

words.definition: A dreamlike state featuring both real and imagined elements.
words.example: To John in his drunken stupor, the party was a wild phantasmagoria of sights and sounds.
words.origin: Early 19th century: probably from French fantasmagorie, from fantasme 'phantasm' plus a fanciful suffix.

words.definition: Self-righteous; holier-than-thou; hypocritical.
words.example: We appreciated Brian's donations to charity, but he lost a lot of our good will by being so loudly pharisaic about them.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from ecclesiastical Latin pharisaicus, from Greek Pharisaios 'Pharisee', from Aramaic perishayya 'separated ones'.

words.definition: A person who is hostile or indifferent to culture and the arts, or who has no understanding of them.
words.example: Ever the philistine,my dad never understood the joy I found in foreign films and classical music.
words.origin: Late 19th century: transferred sense from German Philister, applied by university students to townspeople; ultimately from the biblical Philistines.

words.definition: A lover of beauty; someone who finds and appreciates beauty in all things.
words.example: He considered himself less a critic than a philocalist, always hoping a good movie or book would take him to a place of transcendence.
words.origin: From Greek philos 'loving' and kallos 'beauty'.

words.definition: Not easily excited or upset; calm and composed. In a pejorative sense, apathetic or indifferent.
words.example: Jeremy sat as though lost in thought, an island of phlegmatic cool amid the furor raging around him.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French fleumatique, via Latin from Greek phlegmatikos, referring to phlegm as one of the four bodily humors.

words.definition: Relating to an episodic style of fiction dealing with the adventures of a rough and dishonest but appealing hero.
words.example: Mark Twain's "The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn" is one of the most picaresque novels ever written.
words.origin: Late 18th century: from Spanish picaresco, from picaro 'rogue'.

words.definition: Something trivial.
words.example: Joan's irritation with her employees was picayune compared to the company's more pressing concerns.
words.origin: Early 19th century: from French picaillon 'small coin', from Provencal picaioun, of unknown origin.

words.definition: Trivial nonsense.
words.example: The newspaper was once filled with depth and substance -- now its pages were splashed with piffle about what starlets wore when they went grocery shopping.
words.origin: Late 19th century: of imitative origin.

words.definition: Someone who is small and insignificant.
words.example: Despite his good grades, or perhaps because of them, Andrew was regarded as no more than a pipsqueak by his pals.
words.origin: Early 20th century: imitative, suggesting a small high-pitched sound; military slang in World War I for a small shell.

words.definition: Brief but meaningful. The Gettysburg Address was described as "pithy."
words.example: Her pithy comment lacked the subtlety and misdirection expected in diplomacy.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from pith plus -y, originally meaning 'full of pith'.

words.definition: Having an expressive, melancholy and especially plaintive quality.
words.example: The plangent ballad left no eye in the auditorium dry.
words.origin: Early 19th century: from Latin plangent- 'lamenting', from the verb plangere.

words.definition: A large or excessive amount.
words.example: My parents felt it was best to give me a plethora of advice, often to no avail.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: via late Latin from Greek plethore, from plethein 'be full'.

words.definition: An attitude toward life marked by complacency and a refusal to recognize unpleasant facts.
words.example: As the company collapsed around him, the CEO was marked by a frustrating podsnappery, collecting his bonus with a smile.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from Mr Podsnap, a self-satisfied character in Dickens's Our Mutual Friend.

words.definition: An abject coward.
words.example: That poltroon would run from a butterfly.
words.origin: Early 16th century: from French poltron, from Italian poltrone, perhaps from poltro 'sluggard'.

words.definition: Made up of people or things from different cultures, countries, etc.
words.example: Growing up in a polyglot community, Rick learned at a young age to love French films, Spanish cuisine and the intricacies of the Chinese language.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Greek poluglottos, from polu- 'many' and glotta 'tongue, language'.

words.definition: To speak in a pompous, arrogant or dogmatic manner.
words.example: Susan avoided the blogosphere, considering it primarily a venue in which knaves and naifs could pontificate interminably on their prejudices.
words.origin: Mid 17th century (as a verb in the sense 'be a bishop'): from Latin pontificat-, from the verb pontificare, from pontifex 'high priest'.

words.definition: A person given to vain, pretentious displays and empty chatter; coxcomb; fop.
words.example: Sadly, it behooves us to pay princely commissions to any perfumed popinjay who can open the door to this mystical kingdom.
words.origin: Late Middle English (originally meaning 'parrot'): from Old French papegai, via Spanish from Arabic babagha'.

words.definition: Nonsense. Reportedly from an old Dutch word, 'pappekak,' meaning soft manure.
words.example: That's a load of poppycock, sir! I'd never paint polka dots on your poodle!
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from Dutch dialect pappekak, from pap 'soft' and kak 'dung'.

words.definition: Noun. An indication that something momentous is about to happen; a signal of dire events; an evil omen.
words.example: Here and there, company officers were meeting hastily behind closed doors, and Edmund found these portents ominous and disquieting.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin portentum 'omen, token', from the verb portendere 'foretell'.

words.definition: Ominous. Foreboding. Of great significance.
words.example: Given current trends, 2010 seems an unusually portentous year.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin portentosus, from portentum 'omen, token'.

words.definition: Consummately absurd or foolish.
words.example: Considering his condition, his excuse for returning so late may have seemed reasonable, but to us it was clearly preposterous.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin praeposterus 'reversed, absurd', from prae 'before' and posterus 'coming after'.

words.definition: A sign or warning that something, typically something bad, will happen; an omen or portent.
words.example: He coughed, which in the movies was a presage of doom.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French, from Latin praesagium, from praesagire 'forebode', from prae 'before' and sagire 'perceive keenly'.

words.definition: An intuitive feeling about the future, especially one of foreboding.
words.example: The presentiment caused me to bolt from the plane and rent a car instead.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from obsolete French presentiment, from pre- 'before' and sentiment 'feeling'.

words.definition: Speak or act in an evasive way.
words.example: The politician prevaricated whenever asked about his voting record.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin praevaricat- 'walked crookedly, deviated', from the verb praevaricari, from prae 'before' and varicare 'straddle'.

words.definition: Self-righteously moralistic and superior.
words.example: I walked into the church aware of the priggish deacons casting wary glances at me.
words.origin: Early 18th century: from prig 'self-righteously moralistic person', of unknown origin, plus -ish.

words.definition: Integrity. Honesty.
words.example: The political arena could profit from an adherence to probity by everyone in it.
words.origin: Late 15th century: from French probité or Latin probitas, from probus 'good'.

words.definition: A natural inclination to something; predisposition; propensity.
words.example: Bob had an obvious proclivity for studying turtles, so we were surprised when he became an accountant.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Latin proclivitas, from proclivis 'inclined', from pro- 'forward, down' and clivus 'slope'.

words.definition: Designed to produce conformity to arbitrary standards by ruthless or capriciously violent means. From Procrustes, a mythical giant who stretched or shortened his captives to make them fit their beds.
words.example: My employer's procrustean benefits plan allows paid maternity leaves of only six weeks regardless of the mother's or child's health.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from Procrustes, a robber in Greek mythology who forced victims to fit his bed by stretching or amputating their limbs.

words.definition: Recklessly wasteful; wildly extravagant (adj.).
words.example: The Congress' profligate habits have contributed to our current financial predicament.
words.origin: Early 16th century: from Latin profligatus 'dissolute', past participle of profligare 'overthrow, ruin', from pro- 'forward, down' and fligere 'strike down'.

words.definition: Foretell or prophesy a future event.
words.example: Oscar pundits spent the week prognosticating the results of the upcoming telecast.
words.origin: Early 16th century: from medieval Latin prognosticare, from Latin prognosticum 'sign of the future'.

words.definition: Tending to write or speak tediously and at great length.
words.example: Those who consider Charles Dickens the most prolix of authors in English have never slogged through the sticky thickets of Henry James.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French prolixe or Latin prolixus 'poured forth, extended'.

words.definition: To make known or public; to put into effect by publishing, as a regulation.
words.example: The Office of Government Ethics will promulgate new rules to govern the interaction of lobbyists with political appointees.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin promulgat- 'exposed to public view', from the verb promulgare.

words.definition: Nearness; proximity.
words.example: Your propinquity to the the girl on the park bench suggests more than a passing acquaintance.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin propinquitas, from propinquus 'near'.

words.definition: A rustling or whispering sound, such as leaves in the wind; susurration.
words.example: He left his earbuds at home and took a long walk in the woods, the blathering of podcasts replaced by the soothing soundtrack provided by the psithurism in the trees.
words.origin: From Greek psithurisma 'a whispering', from psithurizein 'to whisper'.

words.definition: Childishly silly and trivial.
words.example: When his old buddies came over, Jake transformed from a respected businessman into an overgrown child, giggling at puerile jokes.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from French puéril or Latin puerilis, from puer 'boy'.

words.definition: Eager or quick to argue, quarrel, or fight.
words.example: Timid in real life, Dave was surprisingly pugnacious on social media.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin pugnax, pugnac- (from pugnare 'to fight', from pugnus 'fist').

words.definition: Attention to minute detail; meticulous.
words.example: Bomb squad members must be punctilious in their work.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from French pointilleux, from pointille, from Italian puntiglio 'small point'.

words.definition: To appropriate wrongfully and often by a breach of trust.
words.example: He used his connections within the organization to purloin a copy of all the passwords to the board's financial accounts.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Anglo-Norman French purloigner 'put away', from Old French loign 'far'.

words.definition: Timid; cowardly.
words.example: He was a pusillanimous man, quick to flee at the slightest hint of danger.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from ecclesiastical Latin pusillanimis, from Latin pusillus 'very small' and animus 'mind'.

words.definition: Commonly believed to be true, but without proof; supposed, reputed.
words.example: For generations Priscilla's family accepted Miles Standish as their putative ancestor.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from late Latin putativus, from Latin putat- 'thought', from the verb putare.

words.definition: Of or characteristic of rotting matter.
words.example: The putrid odor hit my nostrils as soon as I opened the garage, a reminder that I had overlooked garbage day again.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin putridus, from putrere 'to rot', from puter, putr- 'rotten'.

words.definition: To drink deeply or with vigor.
words.example: Ne'er was mingled such a draught / In palace, hall or arbor / As freemen brewed and tyrants quaffed / That night in Boston Harbor. (Oliver Wendell Holmes, American physician, poet and author, 1809-1894)
words.origin: Early 16th century: perhaps imitative, or related to obsolete quass 'drink in large draughts'.

words.definition: Resembling a marsh or quagmire; boggy. Yielding, soft, or flabby.
words.example: The rains turned the backyard into a sloppy, quaggy mess.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from quag (related to quagmire) and the adjectival suffix -y.

words.definition: Overpowered by fatigue; exhausted, or having one's energy consumed.
words.example: After spending all of his Thanksgiving weekend managing the demands of customers, he was positively quanked and slept for 12 hours.
words.origin: English dialectal term of obscure origin, meaning overpowered by fatigue.

words.definition: To defeat, suppress or put down. Most commonly, but not always, used in a legal context.
words.example: I still haven't managed to quash the rumors about how I got this black eye.
words.origin: Middle English: from Old French quasser 'annul', from Latin cassare, from cassus 'null, void'.

words.definition: Petulant; complaining; peevish; whiny.
words.example: Most parents at the meeting had a hunted look, as though they had just escaped homes filled with querulous brats.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from late Latin querulosus, from Latin querulus, from queri 'complain'.

words.definition: Seeker; pursuer.
words.example: He made his way through the woods, careful not to leave any sign for the questrists on his trail.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from quest, from Old French queste, from Latin quaerere 'seek'.

words.definition: A slight objection or criticism.
words.example: The only quibble I had about the play was that the third act went on about five minutes too long.
words.origin: Early 17th century: diminutive of obsolete quib 'a pun', probably from Latin quibus 'to whom'.

words.definition: Whatever makes something the type that it is; essence.
words.example: She was a skilled storyteller, and possessed the ability capture a character's quiddity with just one or two sentences.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from medieval Latin quidditas, from Latin quid 'what'.

words.definition: A person who is eager to know the latest news and gossip; a gossip or busybody.
words.example: The amount of celebrity gossip available 24/7 across all media has turned this culture into a quidnunc's paradise.
words.origin: Early 18th century: from Latin quid nunc? 'what now?'.

words.definition: In a state or period of inactivity or dormancy.
words.example: They hiked past the volcano, which had lay quiescent for nearly a century but had recently begun rumbling to life.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin quiescent- 'being still', from the verb quiescere, from quies 'quiet'.

words.definition: Something that stifles or ends; a period of inactivity.
words.example: Jack Morris' fastball quickly put a quietus to the Yankees' hopes for a late-inning rally.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from medieval Latin quietus est 'he is quit', formula of acquittance.

words.definition: The most perfect or typical example of a quality or class.
words.example: Tom Cruise is the quintessence of the '80s movie star.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French quinte essence, from medieval Latin quinta essentia 'fifth essence'.

words.definition: Traitor; betrayer. After Vidkun Quisling, a Norwegian who collaborated with the Nazis during World War II and was later executed.
words.example: Kathy thought she was Tim's sweetie, but he was secretly playing the quisling with Alice.
words.origin: 1940s: from the name of Major Vidkun Quisling, the Norwegian army officer who collaborated with the Nazis.

words.definition: Excessively romantic; visionary but unrealistic. Like Cervantes' Don Quixote.
words.example: Many cherished ideals of the 1960s now seem more quixotic than even remotely practical.
words.origin: Early 18th century: from Don Quixote, hero of a romance by Cervantes, plus the suffix -ic.

words.definition: A politician whose actions are controlled by someone else.
words.example: For most of his time in office, the congressman was simply a quockerwodger acting at the behest of lobbyists.
words.origin: 19th century: figurative use of quockerwodger, a wooden toy figure jerked into action by a string.

words.definition: Commonplace; occurring daily. Practically nobody uses this word nowadays except Gore Vidal.
words.example: After the excitement of the inauguration, it was a relief to return to the quotidian affairs of ordinary life.
words.origin: Middle English: from Old French cotidien, from Latin quotidianus, from quotidie 'daily'.

words.definition: One who tells stories and anecdotes with skill and wit.
words.example: Garrison Keillor is an accomplished raconteur, so everyone attending his show had high expectations.
words.origin: Early 19th century: French, from raconter 'relate, recount'.

words.definition: Unconventional and slightly disreputable, especially in an attractive manner.
words.example: His raffish manner prompted many warnings from HR, but also made him one of the most intriguing personalities in the office.
words.origin: Early 19th century: from raff (in riff-raff) and the adjectival suffix -ish.

words.definition: Bitterness or resentfulness, especially when long-standing.
words.example: Soon the nation's peace would be replaced by the rancor of election season.
words.origin: Middle English: from Old French rancour, from late Latin rancor 'rancidness', from Latin rancere 'stink'.

words.definition: Cause annoyance or resentment that persists.
words.example: The more I thought about our fight, the more her petty criticisms rankled me.
words.origin: Middle English: from Old French rancler, from draoncle 'festering sore', from medieval Latin dracunculus 'little serpent'.

words.definition: A wild, reckless and sometimes quarrelsome person.
words.example: Their rantipole behavior and constant fighting ruined the party.
words.origin: Late 17th century: of uncertain origin, perhaps from rant and an arbitrary second element.

words.definition: A mischievous person.
words.example: April Fools' Day was better than Christmas for the young rapscallion.
words.origin: Late 17th century: from rascal, by alteration with the suffix -ion.

words.definition: Rational and exact thought, or the process of precise reasoning.
words.example: Logic puzzles require all my powers of ratiocination.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Latin ratiocinatio(n-), from the verb ratiocinari 'to calculate, reason'.

words.definition: A person who speaks confidently but ignorantly.
words.example: My social media feed is filled with rawgabbits trying to explain everything from epidemiology to engineering.
words.origin: Obscure dialectal compound, from raw and gab 'idle talk'.

words.definition: Resisting authority; disobedient; hard to handle.
words.example: Having to ride a recalcitrant, bad-tempered donkey almost ruined my otherwise lovely stay on the island of Santorini.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from Latin recalcitrant- 'kicking out with the heels', from recalcitrare, from calx, calc- 'heel'.

words.definition: One who relapses; a repeat offender.
words.example: The parade of salesmen through my neighborhood was perpetual, like a habitual recidivist in rehab.
words.origin: Late 19th century: from French récidiviste, from Latin recidivus 'falling back', from recidere.

words.definition: (Of a subject or knowledge) little known; abstruse.
words.example: He couldn't pass a history quiz to save his life, but the voracious reader was a repository of facts and recondite information.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin reconditus 'hidden, put away', past participle of recondere.

words.definition: Cowardly; disloyal.
words.example: Unfortunately, her brother James is a recreant liar and backstabber.
words.origin: Middle English: from Old French, present participle of recroire 'surrender, give up', from medieval Latin recredere.

words.definition: A new outbreak, especially of something undesired. Rebirth.
words.example: A great recrudescence of obscurity embraced the boat. The sea in the gulf was as black as the clouds above. (Joseph Conrad, "Nostromo")
words.origin: Late 19th century: from recrudescent, from Latin recrudescere 'become raw again', from re- plus crudus 'raw'.

words.definition: 1. To edit in such a way as to make suitable for publication. 2. To censor sensitive information from a document to protect an individual or institution.
words.example: The agent's name was redacted in published documents to protect his identity.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin redact- 'brought back', from the verb redigere.

words.definition: Reminiscent or suggestive of, like a scent.
words.example: The distillery was filled with a sweet, almost cloying scent, redolent of the farm silos of my youth.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French, or from Latin redolent- 'giving out a strong smell', from re(d)- plus olere 'to smell'.

words.definition: Adj. Radiant; shining brightly. Seeming to shine with light or warmth.
words.example: To Edmund's everlasting delight, the smile the Potato Princess gave him was refulgent with welcome and joy.
words.origin: Late 15th century: from Latin refulgent- 'shining out', from the verb refulgere, from re- plus fulgere 'to shine'.

words.definition: Well-supplied or abounding (with).
words.example: Because of their difficult relationship, the eulogy she gave her mother was replete with backhanded compliments.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French replet or Latin repletus, past participle of replere 'fill up'.

words.definition: Deplorable; guilty; worthy of censure.
words.example: The DEA's efforts in other countries often are hindered by a reprehensible collaboration of law enforcement officers with drug cartels.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from late Latin reprehensibilis, from reprehens- 'rebuked', from the verb reprehendere.

words.definition: The return of hope after a period of despair.
words.example: He woke up the next morning to the sun shining in through the window, igniting the first faint embers of respair in his heart.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from re- 'again' and Old French espeir 'hope', from esperer, from Latin sperare 'to hope'.

words.definition: Splendid, brilliant or glorious.
words.example: Every hillside was resplendent with the dazzling autumn colors of maples and oaks.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin resplendent- 'shining out', from the verb resplendere, from re- plus splendere 'to glitter'.

words.definition: 1. Disposed to be silent or not speak freely; reserved. 2. Reluctant or restrained.
words.example: He proved reticent when asked about his past, not because he had anything to hide, but because he was not given to introspection.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from Latin reticent- 'remaining silent', from the verb reticere, from re- plus tacere 'be silent'.

words.definition: The condition of being lost in thought; daydream.
words.example: Instead of focused on calculations, Jack was in reverie during his math test.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from obsolete French resverie, from Old French reverie 'rejoicing, revelry', from rever 'be delirious'.

words.definition: Coarsely or disrespectfully humorous; vulgar.
words.example: Stand-up comedy has become increasingly ribald since the '60s.
words.origin: Middle English: from Old French ribaud, from riber 'indulge in licentious pleasures', of Germanic origin.

words.definition: An intricate and often petty set of procedures.
words.example: Before I could renew my driver's license I had to endure the usual rigmarole of paperwork.
words.origin: Mid 18th century: apparently an alteration of ragman roll, originally denoting a legal document recording accusations.

words.definition: Split apart, divided into pieces or factions.
words.example: The wood was riven by the strike of the axe.
words.origin: Middle English: from Old Norse rifa 'to tear, rend'.

words.definition: To relax and digest after consuming a large meal.
words.example: After Thanksgiving dinner, I plopped on the couch and rizzled for several hours.
words.origin: English dialectal word of uncertain origin, meaning to relax and digest after a meal.

words.definition: Excessively ornate, elaborate or intricate; florid.
words.example: "He worked up a prose so rococo that one could have built grottoes out of it." (The New Yorker's Brendan Gill, describing 1930s newspaper columnist Lucius Beebe.)
words.origin: Early 19th century: from French, humorously formed from rocaille 'rockwork, shell-work'.

words.definition: A plant growing on waste ground or among refuse.
words.example: The ruderal in the midst of the parking lot was a reminder that life could endure in the most desolate places.
words.origin: Late 19th century: from modern Latin ruderalis, from Latin rudus, ruder- 'rubble'.

words.definition: Good judgment; sense, intelligence.
words.example: He went to his grandfather for his advice, knowing his rumblegumption had served him well over his long life.
words.origin: 18th century Scots dialect: from rumble and gumption 'common sense, shrewdness'.

words.definition: A noisy, confused or disruptive commotion.
words.example: I entered the daycare, wondering how the teachers held onto their sanity during the daily rumpus.
words.origin: Mid 18th century: probably a fanciful formation, of uncertain origin.

words.definition: A runaway, fugitive or deserter.
words.example: Police searched every house, shack and doghouse for the runagate.
words.origin: Late Middle English: alteration of renegate (later renegade), influenced by run and obsolete agate 'on the way'.

words.definition: Sweet or sentimental in a way that does not seem sincere or genuine.
words.example: My mom was moved by the film, but I found it to be a saccharine mess.
words.origin: Early 19th century: from Latin saccharum 'sugar', from Greek sakkharon.

words.definition: Sacred; beyond criticism; inviolable.
words.example: Every writer thinks his prose is sacrosanct -- until he meets a really good editor.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin sacrosanctus, from sacro 'by a sacred rite' and sanctus 'holy'.

words.definition: Keen discernment or insight; sound judgment.
words.example: He proposed that people were easier to educate if they had a natural sagacity to start with.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin sagacitas, from sagax 'wise'.

words.definition: Prominent; most important (adj.) Projection (n.), as a military position protruding into enemy lines.
words.example: Among the jumble of comments about the value of diversity, one proved solidly salient.
words.origin: Mid 16th century (in the sense 'leaping'): from Latin salient- 'leaping', from the verb salire.

words.definition: Health-giving; healthy.
words.example: He loved camping; the fresh air and sunshine created a salubrious atmosphere that rejuvenated him after a week in the office.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin salubris (from salus 'health') plus -ous.

words.definition: Producing good effects; beneficial.
words.example: The new drug had a salutary effect on his back pain.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from French salutaire or Latin salutaris, from salus 'health'.

words.definition: (sahn frwah') In French, literally "cold blood." Imperturbability; great calm.
words.example: Roger took his firing with the utmost sangfroid, silently cleaning out his desk and leaving unobtrusively by the back door.
words.origin: Mid 18th century: French, literally 'cold blood'.

words.definition: 1. Optimistic, cheerful, confident. 2. Of the color of blood. ... In medieval physiology, a reddish complexion was thought to imply a hopeful temperament.
words.example: Andy was sanguine about the Rams' chances against the hapless 49ers.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French sanguin(e) 'blood red', from Latin sanguineus 'of blood', from sanguis 'blood'.

words.definition: Scornfully mocking or cynical; disdainfully humorous.
words.example: Sardonic laughter was his only reaction to the suggestion that he liked to knit frilly sweaters.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from French sardonique, from Greek sardonios 'of Sardinia', denoting a plant which produced facial convulsions resembling laughter when eaten.

words.definition: Of or relating to tailoring, clothes or style of dress.
words.example: He was dressed to the nines and beaming with sartorial elegance.
words.origin: Early 19th century: from Latin sartor 'tailor' (from sarcire 'to patch') plus -ial.

words.definition: Gloomy, cold and unfeeling, morose, sardonic.
words.example: When Alec took off his clown suit he seemed instantly to become his old sullen and saturnine self.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French saturnin, from medieval Latin Saturninus 'of Saturn', the planet thought to bring a gloomy temperament.

words.definition: Impertinent; impudent.
words.example: She was a saucy wench, and I readily imagined the piquant delights of living with her quick wits for the rest of my years.
words.origin: Late 15th century: from sauce plus -y, with the original sense 'savoury'.

words.definition: A person who behaves badly but in an amusingly mischievous rather than harmful way; a rascal.
words.example: Bart Simpson may be the most beloved scalawag in the United States.
words.origin: Mid 19th century (originally US): of unknown origin.

words.definition: A cowardly buffoon.
words.example: He talked a big game on social media, but the scaramouch was no help when it came time to put plans into action.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Italian Scaramuccia, the name of a stock cowardly braggart character in commedia dell'arte.

words.definition: (SHA den froy duh. A noun borrowed shamelessly from German.) Pleasure or satisfaction derived from someone else's misfortune.
words.example: Those of us who weathered the barbs of Spiro Agnew were treated to an exquisite taste of schadenfreude at his downfall.
words.origin: Late 19th century: from German Schaden 'harm' and Freude 'joy'.

words.definition: A very, very small amount.
words.example: There was not a scintilla of evidence to support the allegation of perjury.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin, literally 'spark'.

words.definition: Sparkling or shining brightly.
words.example: We sat on the beach, the setting sun giving the water a scintillating shimmer.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin scintillat- 'sparkled', from the verb scintillare, from scintilla 'spark'.

words.definition: Having a strong urge to write.
words.example: The solitude made him scripturient, and he spent the four days filling up an old journal with thoughts, memories and hopes for the future.
words.origin: 17th century: from Latin scripturire 'to desire to write', from scribere 'to write'.

words.definition: Morally degenerate; corrupt.
words.example: The governor is a typically scrofulous Illinois politician.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from medieval Latin scrofulosus, from scrofulae, the disease scrofula; later extended to mean morally corrupt.

words.definition: Humorously insulting.
words.example: I loved reading Roger Ebert's scurrilous pans of the movies he hated.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin scurrilis (from scurra 'buffoon') plus -ous.

words.definition: To hastily tidy a house.
words.example: Upon receiving the call that their parents were due home two hours earlier than planned, the kids scurryfunged in an attempt to hide evidence of their weekend party.
words.origin: 19th-century dialectal English: of uncertain origin, perhaps from scurry plus dialectal funge.

words.definition: A versatile verb meaning to sink a ship or boat deliberately; to sink figuratively, as to scuttle a project; or to scurry.
words.example: In 1939, the Germans scuttled the pocket battleship "Graf Spee" to keep the British from capturing her. ... OR ... When we turned on the light, mice scuttled under the furniture.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: probably from obsolete skottle, from Spanish escotilla 'hatchway'.

words.definition: An eloquently and obstinately argumentative person.
words.example: He was one of those mouthy sea lawyers, full of pseudo-intellectual yammer.
words.origin: 18th century: nautical slang, from sea plus lawyer, denoting a contentious sailor.

words.definition: A person who remains a resident of the place or region of his birth
words.example: She was a sedens of Port Chester who never viewed the spires of a metropolitan skyline.
words.origin: From Latin sedens 'sitting, remaining', present participle of sedere 'to sit'.

words.definition: Unusual, strange.
words.example: He exited the spaceship, his heart pounding in anticipation of the selcouth wonders awaiting him on the planet.
words.origin: Old English selcūth, from seldan 'seldom' and cūth 'known'.

words.definition: Given to moralizing in a pompous or affected manner.
words.example: Each time I ended up in trouble, I endured a sententious lecture from the principal, who saw himself as some sort of guru appointed to keep me on track.
words.origin: Late Middle English (in the sense 'full of meaning'): from Latin sententiosus, from sententia 'opinion'.

words.definition: A small room or monument, cut in rock or built of stone, in which a dead person is laid or buried.
words.example: The archaeologist was the first person to step inside the sepulchre in two millennia.
words.origin: Middle English: from Old French sepulcre, from Latin sepulcrum 'burial place', from sepelire 'to bury'.

words.definition: Taking one subject after another in regular order; point by point.
words.example: I will address the issues you raised seriatim.
words.origin: Late 15th century: medieval Latin, from Latin series 'series', on the pattern of words such as gradatim.

words.definition: Having many syllables.
words.example: The article's collection of sesquipedalian words couldn't hide the columnist's empty thoughts.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin sesquipedalis 'a foot and a half long', from sesqui- 'one and a half' and pes, ped- 'foot'.

words.definition: In a loose, disorderly manner.
words.example: He woke up too late to shower or shave and appeared shackbaggerly at the morning meeting.
words.origin: Dialectal English: of uncertain origin, perhaps from shack 'to shake about' plus bagger.

words.definition: Secret or dishonest activity or maneuvering.
words.example: He sat down on the couch and turned on the television, but noise from the kitchen told him the kids were up to some shenanigans around the cookie jar.
words.origin: Mid 19th century (originally US): of unknown origin.

words.definition: Producing a hissing sound, like that of "s" or "sh."
words.example: ..."the tinkle of the bells, the immediate sibilance of rubber heels and starched skirts, the querulous murmur of voices..." (William Faulkner, "The Wild Palms")
words.origin: From Latin sibilare 'to hiss, whistle', of imitative origin.

words.definition: A position or commission that requires little or no work or real responsibility but still receives payment.
words.example: Edmund deserved to be fired, but his uncle was CEO and gave him a sinecure in the mailroom instead.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from medieval Latin (beneficium) sine cura '(benefice) without cure (of souls)'.

words.definition: A small piece of bread or toast used to dip into soup or sauce or as a garnish.
words.example: The chili was served with a cornbread sippet.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: probably a diminutive of sop.

words.definition: To allure, especially by melodious singing.
words.example: When she took the stage, she sirenized the entire room.
words.origin: From Siren, the sea nymphs of Greek mythology whose singing lured sailors to destruction, plus -ize.

words.definition: To run away; flee.
words.example: The children had to skedaddle from the old man's lawn when he yelled at them.
words.origin: Mid 19th century (originally US, in military use): of unknown origin.

words.definition: A person who spends as little money as possible; a miser.
words.example: The old skinflint wore the same ragged, torn coat for years just to avoid buying a new one.
words.origin: Late 17th century: from skin plus flint, from the notion of skinning a flint to save money.

words.definition: Underhanded or unscrupulous behavior. Trickery.
words.example: The most successful political strategists often are those adept at skullduggery.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: alteration of Scots sculduddery, of unknown origin.

words.definition: Quench or satisfy (one's thirst).
words.example: The icy water slaked his thirst after the eight-mile hike under the summer sun.
words.origin: Old English slacian 'become less eager', also 'slacken', from slæc 'slack'.

words.definition: One who uses abusive slang; a ranting partisan.
words.example: While it started as a way to keep Americans informed, cable news quickly devolved into a collection of angry slangwhangers.
words.origin: Early 19th century (originally US): from slang plus whang 'to beat noisily'.

words.definition: (Typically of a person or method of work) characterized by a lack of care, thought, or organization.
words.example: The deck was a slipshod construction that looked like it would crumble the second someone set foot on it.
words.origin: Late 16th century (originally meaning 'wearing slippers or loose shoes'): from slip plus shod.

words.definition: To swallow greedily.
words.example: He slonked down his spaghetti as if he hadn't had a meal in three years.
words.origin: Dialectal English: of imitative or uncertain origin.

words.definition: A dirty rascal.
words.example: An army of slubberdegullions sat around the bar, slobbering and babbling.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from dialectal slubber 'to smear, do carelessly' plus a fanciful suffix.

words.definition: A lazy person who stays in bed late.
words.example: He wanted to get up early and exercise, but the slugabed slept past his alarm and woke up five minutes before his exam.
words.origin: Early 16th century: from slug 'be lazy' plus a bed.

words.definition: A cheap or insubstantial stew.
words.example: He was hungry enough to devour a steak the size of a steering wheel. But payday was still over a week off, so he settled for a bowl of slumgullion at a dive bar down the road.
words.origin: Mid 19th century (originally US): of unknown origin.

words.definition: Overly, even unbearably, ingratiating. Unctuous.
words.example: I couldn't talk any longer to the smarmy jerk trying to sell me a car.
words.origin: Early 20th century: from dialectal smarm 'to smear, plaster down' plus -y.

words.definition: 1. Impressive, marvelous or very effective. 2. Devastating or crushing.
words.example: Through a smashing bit of swordplay, Errol dealt his opponent in epee a smashing defeat.
words.origin: From smash plus -ing; the colloquial sense 'excellent' arose in the late 19th century.

words.definition: A hole in a hedge or wall.
words.example: The cat stopped at the edge of the yard and then sauntered through the smeuse to see what the neighbors had to offer.
words.origin: Dialectal English (southern counties): of uncertain origin, related to a hole made by the repeated passage of a small animal.

words.definition: A drizzly fog or mist.
words.example: Nothing added to the frustration of a Monday morning like driving through the smur to get to the office.
words.origin: Scots and northern English dialect: of uncertain origin, perhaps imitative.

words.definition: A shrewd, unprincipled person, especially a politician.
words.example: The tone of national politics changed when humble public servants were replaced by power-hungry snollygosters.
words.origin: Mid 19th century (originally US): of uncertain origin, perhaps an alteration of snallygaster, a mythical monster.

words.definition: Having an attractive or pleasing face.
words.example: His character might leave much to be desired, but his snoutfair appearance made him a hit on TV.
words.origin: 16th century: from snout plus fair 'beautiful', literally 'fair of face'.

words.definition: A person's nickname.
words.example: The curmudgeonly parks director moonlighted as a smooth-talking saxophonist with the sobriquet Duke Silver.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: French, of unknown origin.

words.definition: Something that settles a matter; a decisive blow or answer.
words.example: On the playground, "I know you are, but what am I" is the ultimate sockdolager to many an argument.
words.origin: Early 19th century (originally US): of unknown origin, perhaps an alteration of doxology.

words.definition: Resembling or characteristic of a sleepwalker; sluggish.
words.example: The Monday after Daylight Saving Time began, the office parking lot was filled with somnombulant employees doing their best to lurch to work.
words.origin: Early 19th century: from Latin somnus 'sleep' and ambulare 'to walk'.

words.definition: Having an attractive and healthy appearance.
words.example: He returned from vacation vibrant and sonsy; the time outdoors had done him well.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Scots and Irish sonse 'abundance, good fortune', from Scottish Gaelic sonas 'good fortune'.

words.definition: To walk in a slow, leisurely manner.
words.example: He soodled down the boardwalk, taking in the ocean view and having not a care in the world.
words.origin: Dialectal English: of uncertain origin, perhaps a variant of saunter.

words.definition: A plausible but fallacious or misleading argument.
words.example: "Abuse of words has been the great instrument of sophistry and chicanery, of party, faction and division of society." (John Adams, second U.S. president)
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French sophistrie, from medieval Latin sophistria, from Latin sophista 'sophist'.

words.definition: To imbue with moral principles or self-control.
words.example: Parents have an obligation to sophronize their children.
words.origin: From Greek sophronizein 'to make wise or moderate', from sophron 'of sound mind, prudent'.

words.definition: To dress, split and butterfly a fowl for roasting or grilling.
words.example: This year Mom spatchcocked her Thanksgiving turkey and cooked it in half the usual time. By the end of the Lions game, Brian had drunk so much that he was laid out like he'd been spatchcocked, too.
words.origin: Late 18th century: probably from dispatch plus cock, denoting a fowl killed and cooked in haste.

words.definition: Having the ring of truth or plausibility but actually fallacious or at best not serious.
words.example: His argument was convincing, but most of us knew -- as he did -- that it was specious.
words.origin: Late Middle English (in the sense 'beautiful'): from Latin speciosus 'fair', from species 'appearance'.

words.definition: Smart or showy in appearance; dapper.
words.example: He was known for his gallantry and sprauncy dress.
words.origin: Early 20th century: of uncertain origin, perhaps a variant of 'spruce'.

words.definition: Not being what it purports to be; false or fake.
words.example: The blogger was known to make spurious claims just to drive up traffic on his site.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin spurius 'false', literally 'illegitimate child'.

words.definition: A noisy altercation or quarrel usually over petty matters.
words.example: When she heard him get into a squabble with his friends over "Star Trek" trivia, Kim knew it was time to find a new boyfriend.
words.origin: Early 17th century: probably of Scandinavian origin and imitative.

words.definition: Extremely dirty and unpleasant, especially as a result of poverty or neglect.
words.example: The basement was a squalid mess, with stacks of old magazines and used clothes collecting mold in a corner.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Latin squalidus, from squalere 'be rough or dirty'.

words.definition: A soft sucking sound made when pressure is applied to liquid or mud.
words.example: They trudged through the woods, the squelch of their boots in the mud the only sound for miles.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: imitative of the sound.

words.definition: (Of a person's voice) Loud and powerful.
words.example: He took to the podium, puffed out his chest and delivered his speech in a commanding, stentorian baritone.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Stentor, a Greek herald in the Iliad with a powerful voice.

words.definition: Restrain (a reaction) or stop oneself acting on (an emotion).
words.example: He peeked at his phone during church and stifled a giggle when he saw his friend's text message.
words.origin: Late Middle English: perhaps from Old French estouffer 'smother, stifle'.

words.definition: To roam or wander aimlessly.
words.example: He left the subway station and spent hours stravaging around the city, gawking at the tall buildings and crowds of people.
words.origin: 18th century: Scots and northern English contraction of 'extravagate' 'wander off'.

words.definition: Cause to lose enthusiasm and initiative, especially as a result of a tedious or restrictive routine.
words.example: The stultifying file work robbed the young intern of the joy she'd shown on the first day.
words.origin: Early 18th century: from late Latin stultificare, from Latin stultus 'foolish'.

words.definition: Exceedingly dark and gloomy; hellish. From the Styx, the mythological river boundary between Earth and Hades.
words.example: The blackness that enveloped Bert and Ernie when the lantern went out was so stygian and oppressive that it threw them into a panic.
words.origin: Late 16th century: via Latin from Greek Stugios, from Stux, Stug- 'the river Styx'.

words.definition: Prevent or hinder the progress of.
words.example: The rain persisted all week and stymied my attempts to go for a run.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: originally a golf term, of unknown origin.

words.definition: To induce secretly to do an unlawful thing.
words.example: He suborned the assistant to delete the incriminating files.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin subornare 'incite secretly', from sub- 'secretly' plus ornare 'equip'.

words.definition: Monotonous urban sprawl of standardized buildings.
words.example: As the city grew outward, our charming small town became a subtopia overrun with franchise pharmacies and strip malls.
words.origin: 1950s: blend of 'suburb' and 'utopia', coined by Ian Nairn.

words.definition: Assistance and support in times of hardship and distress.
words.example: She never hesitated to provide succor to her fellow churchgoers after sickness or funerals.
words.origin: Middle English: from Old French sucurs, from medieval Latin succursus, from Latin succurrere 'run to the help of'.

words.definition: Outmoded, old-fashioned.
words.example: Although he was popular in high school, his sense of style and grasp of trends made him superannuated by the time he hit 30.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from medieval Latin superannuatus, from Latin super- 'over' plus annus 'year'.

words.definition: Contemptuous, arrogant or condescending.
words.example: I knew I was about to go into the tank socially when I noticed the supercilious way she looked at my red shoes.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin superciliosus, from supercilium 'eyebrow'.

words.definition: More than enough. Unecessary. Extraneous. Redundant.
words.example: I edited her narrative down to 240 words just by removing the superfluous twaddle.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin superfluus, from super- 'over' plus fluere 'to flow'.

words.definition: Excess; overabundance.
words.example: Edmund lived on an attractive street, but a surfeit of yappy dogs in surrounding yards almost drove him crazy.
words.origin: Middle English: from Old French sorfait, past participle of sorfaire 'overdo'.

words.definition: A murmuring or whispering sound. A classic example of onomatopoeia, from the Latin for "whisper."
words.example: As Alice sat by the window with her gin and tonic, she could hear the comforting susurrus of the sea far away.
words.origin: Early 19th century: from Latin susurrus 'a whisper', of imitative origin.

words.definition: A state of nervous excitement or extreme agitation.
words.example: We'd worked ourselves into such a swivet over the proposed staff cuts that it was almost a letdown when they failed to materialize.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: of unknown origin, originally US dialect.

words.definition: Overwhelmingly hot, boiling and humid weather.
words.example: He stepped outside, bracing himself for the swullocking August day.
words.origin: English dialect (chiefly Sussex): related to 'swullock', perhaps imitative of close, oppressive heat.

words.definition: Fond of sensuous luxury or pleasure; self-indulgent.
words.example: As soon as the kids were out of the house, Dan cashed out his savings and had a sybaritic retirement on the Florida coast.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Sybaris, an ancient Greek city in Italy noted for its luxury.

words.definition: Someone who seeks personal advantage by excessively trying to please someone else. Toady; brown-noser; suck-up.
words.example: "Princes were always at the mercy of others and ready to lend their ears to sycophants." (Mohandas K. Gandhi, 1869-1948, Indian political and spiritual leader)
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin sycophanta, from Greek sukophantēs 'informer'.

words.definition: A mythical being like a sprite or fairy.
words.example: She caught his eye from across the ballroom, moving like a sylph as she greeted the guests.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from modern Latin sylphes (plural), perhaps coined by Paracelsus.

words.definition: Consisting of or associated with woods; wooded.
words.example: He stepped out of the tent and breathed deep, taking in the fresh air of the sylvan setting.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from French sylvain or Latin silva 'a wood'.

words.definition: The nearly straight-line configuration of three celestial bodies in a gravitational system.
words.example: He stared at the lunar eclipse, a perfect szygy of the sun, Earth and the moon.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: via late Latin from Greek suzugia, from suzugos 'yoked, paired'.

words.definition: Habitually silent or reserved; reticent, uncommunicative.
words.example: The prime minister was quite taciturn in today's meeting, obscuring the emotional turmoil we knew he was feeling.
words.origin: Mid 18th century: from Latin taciturnus, from tacitus 'silent'.

words.definition: A petty lie.
words.example: Every Monday morning, he was equipped with a new taradiddle about his weekend adventures, even though we knew he spent it on the couch playing video games.
words.origin: Late 18th century: of unknown origin.

words.definition: A person who never drinks alcohol.
words.example: A teetotaler, he never joined his coworkers for drinks after a long week.
words.origin: Early 19th century: from teetotal, with emphatic reduplication of the initial letter of 'total'.

words.definition: Reckless boldness; audacity.
words.example: Hermione talked back to Professor Snape with what the Slytherins called cheekiness, but the Gryffindors took pride in her temerity.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin temeritas, from temere 'rashly'.

words.definition: Make conflicting or evasive statements; equivocate.
words.example: In the course of three days, the political candidate tergiversated on union workers' rights when addressing different audiences.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Latin tergiversari 'turn one's back', from tergum 'back' plus vertere 'to turn'.

words.definition: A quarrelsome, overbearing woman; a shrew.
words.example: She was the perfect termagant, slamming her fried chicken on the table and squabbling with her husband all through dinner.
words.origin: Middle English: from Tervagant, the name of an imaginary deity in medieval mystery plays.

words.definition: Slavery; servitude.
words.example: Better to live free than be in thrall to an overbearing master -- or any sort of master, for that matter!
words.origin: Old English thrǣl 'slave', from Old Norse thrǣll.

words.definition: A heavy fall of rain during a thunderstorm.
words.example: He'd hoped he could get home before the storm got too bad, but found himself instead jogging through a soggy thunderplump.
words.origin: Scots and northern English: from 'thunder' plus 'plump' (a sudden heavy fall).

words.definition: A light meal, especially lunch.
words.example: He worked all day, breaking only to eat a meager tiffin at his computer before jumping back in.
words.origin: Early 19th century (Anglo-Indian): apparently from dialect tiffing 'sipping', of unknown origin.

words.definition: A ringing or tinkling sound.
words.example: As he entered the town, he was greeted by a tintinnabulation from the church bell tower, welcoming him home.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from Latin tintinnabulum 'bell', from tintinnare 'to ring', of imitative origin.

words.definition: A silly act, matter, or thing; foolish or silly behavior.
words.example: "Stop this tomfoolery!" Mrs. Baldwin yelled, but her students continued to fill the air with paper airplanes.
words.origin: Early 19th century: from Tom Fool (a name for a half-witted person) plus -ery.

words.definition: A part of a monk's or priest's head left bare on top by shaving off the hair.
words.example: His hair was thinning, and he'd soon have a tonsure to rival Fr. Callahan's.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin tonsura, from tondere 'to shear, clip'.

words.definition: Agreeable, attractive.
words.example: Looking at their old wedding pictures, Jack discovered his parents weren't always the chubby, badly dressed embarrassments he felt they were. They actually made a rather toothsome couple.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from tooth plus -some, originally meaning 'pleasant to the taste'.

words.definition: Mentally or physically inactive; lethargic.
words.example: The torpid teen sat on the couch shoveling chips into his mouth, his eyes never breaking from Cartoon Network.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin torpidus, from torpere 'be numb or sluggish'.

words.definition: Regarded as being symbolic or representative of a particular quality or concept.
words.example: Indiana Jones is a totemic hero of the cinema.
words.origin: Early 19th century: from totem, from Ojibwa nindoodem 'my totem'.

words.definition: Right away; immediately.
words.example: Morris, if you don't stop tormenting your sister and come inside tout de suite, there's going to be big trouble.
words.origin: French, literally 'all sweet (i.e. continuous)', meaning 'at once'.

words.definition: A person fond of or expert at archery.
words.example: His path to becoming a toxophilite began the afternoon he picked up a bow and arrow at summer camp.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Greek toxon 'bow' plus philos 'loving', popularized by Roger Ascham's 'Toxophilus'.

words.definition: Easily controlled, managed or led.
words.example: "As long as the King of France treats me gently he will find me as gentle and tractable as he can desire; but if he be rough, I shall take the trouble to be just as troublesome and offensive to him as I can." Elizabeth I, Queen of England (1533-1603)
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin tractabilis, from tractare 'to handle'.

words.definition: To change completely, often grotesquely, in appearance and form.
words.example: So Gregor drifted off to sleep, never dreaming he was in a Kafka story and would transmogrify into a hideous insect overnight.
words.origin: Late 16th century: of unknown origin, perhaps a fanciful coinage based on 'transform'.

words.definition: Cloying sentimentality or flattery.
words.example: The film was blatant treacle, an attempt to have viewers reaching for tissues every five minutes.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French triacle, via Latin from Greek thēriakē 'antidote against venom'.

words.definition: Vigorous or incisive in expression or style.
words.example: The critic made a trenchant argument for the movie's awfulness.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French, literally 'cutting', present participle of trenchier 'to cut'.

words.definition: A brief space of time.
words.example: The squirrel appeared on the lawn and the dog was after it in a trice.
words.origin: Late Middle English (as a verb meaning 'pull, hoist'): from Middle Dutch trisen 'pull sharply'.

words.definition: Forget that old first definition; the second is more useful: Rubbish; junk; something tawdry or worthless.
words.example: Phil was appalled at the souvenir stands, fast-food joints and other tripe that blocked his view of the ocean.
words.origin: Middle English: from Old French, of unknown ultimate origin.

words.definition: Literally, a cave-dweller. More frequently, a backward, mentally sluggish person.
words.example: Emily felt she could have saved the company if only the troglodytes in management had taken her advice.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: via Latin from Greek trōglodutēs, from trōglē 'hole' plus duein 'go into'.

words.definition: To submit obsequiously; be subservient; kowtow.
words.example: When I'm in the presence of a powerful person, my own concept of equality gets blurry and I have a regrettable tendency to truckle, if only to be polite. (Ian Frazier, "Travels in Siberia," 2010)
words.origin: Late 16th century: from 'truckle bed', the lower bed slept in by a servant; hence 'take a subordinate position'.

words.definition: Savage; pugnacious; ferocious; brutally harsh; defiant.
words.example: Alex's astonishingly truculent response to my mild reproof made me think he might be nursing some secret grudge.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: from Latin truculentus, from trux, truc- 'fierce'.

words.definition: A loud, confused noise, especially one caused by a large mass of people.
words.example: A large tumult rang out, attracting the attention of police and media.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin tumultus, related to tumere 'to swell'.

words.definition: Excessively ornate or (more likely) pompous and overblown language.
words.example: Readers of many Internet blogs are subjected to considerable turgidity.
words.origin: From turgid, from Latin turgidus, from turgere 'to swell'.

words.definition: Depravity, wickedness.
words.example: The trial exposed the public to the turpitude hiding behind his pleasant demeanor.
words.origin: Late 15th century: from French, or from Latin turpitudo, from turpis 'disgraceful, base'.

words.definition: Trivial or foolish speech or writing; nonsense.
words.example: Max sighed as he turned to the paper's editorial page, where his father had submitted another rambling piece of twaddle espousing his political views.
words.origin: Mid 18th century: variant of the obsolete 'twattle', perhaps influenced by 'twaddle' of imitative origin.

words.definition: To twang with the fingers on a musical instrument.
words.example: He sat on he porch as dusk rolled in, twankling an old childhood favorite on the banjo.
words.origin: English dialect: frequentative of 'twang', of imitative origin.

words.definition: Excessively cute; nice to the point of being saccharine.
words.example: The Teletubbies are somewhat more twee than I can bear.
words.origin: Early 20th century: representing a childish pronunciation of 'sweet'.

words.definition: A beginner in learning; a novice.
words.example: To Beth's everlasting amusement, John had been a perfect tyro at seduction.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin tiro 'young soldier, recruit'.

words.definition: Frightful, loathsome.
words.example: The congressman resigned after his ugsome photos were discovered on Instagram.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old Norse ugga 'to dread' plus the suffix -some.

words.definition: The experience of waking up before dawn and lying awake, consumed by anxious thoughts or worries.
words.example: She woke up while it was still dark and lay there for hours in a state of uhtceare over unpaid bills and mounting responsibilities.
words.origin: Old English: from uht 'period before dawn' and cearu 'care, sorrow'.

words.definition: Expressing opinions on matters outside the scope of one's knowledge or expertise.
words.example: Many who haven't had kids are too eager to give ultracrepidarian advice to parents.
words.origin: Early 19th century: from Latin ultra crepidam 'beyond the sole', alluding to the painter Apelles' rebuke of a cobbler who criticized more than footwear.

words.definition: A feeling of pique or resentment at some often fancied slight or insult.
words.example: She took umbrage at his snide remarks.
words.origin: Late Middle English (denoting shade): from Old French ombrage, from Latin umbra 'shadow'.

words.definition: Oily or greasy; unpleasantly polite and insincerely earnest.
words.example: The mediator was so unctuous that both sides found him impossible to work with.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from medieval Latin unctuosus, from Latin unctus 'anointing', from unguere 'anoint'.

words.definition: Unedited; without deletions.
words.example: I prefer the unexpurgated edition of the movie; it's longer but more exciting.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from un- plus expurgate, from Latin expurgare 'cleanse', from ex- 'out' and purgare 'purify'.

words.definition: To an exorbitant degree. Adverb form of usurious, or "excessively immoderate."
words.example: Some people think gasoline prices in the Atlanta area have stayed usuriously high.
words.origin: From usurious, from medieval Latin usurarius, from Latin usura 'usury', plus the adverbial suffix -ly.

words.definition: The illegal action or practice of lending money at unreasonably high rates of interest.
words.example: He made a fortune through usury, while many of his clients lost all they had.
words.origin: Middle English: from medieval Latin usuria, from Latin usura, from usus 'use', from uti 'to use'.

words.definition: Excessively devoted or submissive to one's wife.
words.example: Bill was so uxorious that he never got to play poker with us on the weekends.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from Latin uxorius, from uxor 'wife'.

words.definition: Lacking in intelligence or ideas. Devoid of meaning.
words.example: "Television was not invented to make human beings vacuous, but is an emanation of their vacuity." (Malcolm Muggeridge, journalist, 1903-1990)
words.origin: Mid 17th century (in the sense 'empty of matter'): from Latin vacuus 'empty' plus -ous.

words.definition: Excessive or ostentatious pride, especially in one's achievements.
words.example: He was filled with vainglory, constantly reminding everyone of his perfect GPA and lengthy resume.
words.origin: Middle English: from Old French veine gloire, from Latin vana gloria 'empty glory'.

words.definition: Depart hurriedly.
words.example: The neighbor kid always made a point to vamoose before he had to help clean up the mess.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from Spanish vamos 'let us go'.

words.definition: Not lively or interesting. Dull or boring.
words.example: After a long day at the office, Tom didn't want to read anything overly taxing or deep; he was happy to settle for vapid reality TV.
words.origin: Mid 17th century (used of a drink in the sense 'lacking flavour'): from Latin vapidus.

words.definition: Full of folly; senseless, crazy.
words.example: Everyone in Hollywood thought Walt Disney's plan to make a feature-length animated film was a vecordious scheme.
words.origin: From Latin vecors, vecordis 'senseless, mad', from ve- 'without' and cor 'heart, mind'.

words.definition: A wish or inclination not strong enough to lead to action.
words.example: Greg imagined getting off the couch and running a marathon, but his desire for exercise remained a velleity.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from medieval Latin velleitas, from Latin velle 'to wish'.

words.definition: Capable of being bought; open to bribery, especially as a violation of trust.
words.example: In my hometown it was generally accepted that the city council was made up of venal men whose votes and influence were available to the highest bidder.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin venalis 'for sale', from venum 'thing for sale'.

words.definition: A thin coating of ice or frozen rain on an exposed surface.
words.example: A sheet of verglas covered the branches, making them sparkle dangerously in the morning light.
words.origin: Late 19th century: from French verglas, from verre 'glass' and glas 'ice'.

words.definition: The appearance or quality of being true or real.
words.example: Though winsome and fascinating, the legend of King Arthur lacks historical verisimilitude.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin verisimilitudo, from verisimilis 'probable', from veri 'truly' and similis 'like'.

words.definition: Evening.
words.example: He sat on the porch, took a deep breath and sat in the vesper light.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Latin vesper 'evening, evening star'.

words.definition: A visible sign or trace remaining after something more important has disappeared. 2. In biology, surviving in a degenerate, atrophied or imperfect form.
words.example: She occasionally glimpsed vestigial remnants of the grand Detroit that used to be. ... OR ... Some primitive snakes still bear signs of vestigial limbs.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from Latin vestigium 'footprint, trace' plus -al.

words.definition: To make someone feel annoyed, frustrated or worried, especially with trivial matters.
words.example: I'm fairly sure the process at the DMV was designed to vex even the most saintly of people.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old French vexer, from Latin vexare 'shake, disturb'.

words.definition: Changes, especially in life circumstances or fortunes; changes as a natural process. Usually seen as plural.
words.example: It takes a strong love indeed to weather the inherent vicissitudes of life.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from French vicissitude or Latin vicissitudo, from vicissim 'by turns', from vicis 'turn, change'.

words.definition: Food or provisions.
words.example: Before heading off on the hike, they stopped at the general store to stock up on victuals.
words.origin: Middle English: from Old French vitaille, from late Latin victualia, neuter plural of victualis, from victus 'food'.

words.definition: Instinctive, as opposed to rational, like a "gut feeling." From the Latin "viscera," for internal organs.
words.example: She felt an almost visceral obligation to help the homeless.
words.origin: Late 16th century: from medieval Latin visceralis, from Latin viscera 'internal organs'.

words.definition: Bitter and abusive.
words.example: What began as a cordial political discussion soon gave way to vituperative attacks on my character.
words.origin: Early 18th century: from Latin vituperat- 'censured', from the verb vituperare, plus -ive.

words.definition: Clamorous; loud; making or given to noisy outcries.
words.example: Unfortunately, the victory celebration turned into a field day for vociferous, boisterous slobs.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin vociferari 'exclaim', from vox 'voice' and ferre 'to bear', plus -ous.

words.definition: Having a very eager approach to an activity.
words.example: The voracious reader never went anywhere without at least two books to keep him company.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Latin vorax, voracis (from vorare 'devour') plus -ious.

words.definition: Crafty, cunning.
words.example: The salesman turned around, a vulpine gleam in his eye, and I could almost hear my bank account deflate.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from Latin vulpinus, from vulpes 'fox'.

words.definition: (Of a cruel or violent action) deliberate and unprovoked.
words.example: Every day, he had to endure the wanton taunts and threats of the school's bully.
words.origin: Middle English wantowen 'undisciplined', from wan- 'badly' and Old English togen 'trained'.

words.definition: (Of a bird) sing softly and with a succession of constantly changing notes.
words.example: Jake sat on a log and enjoyed the stillness, which was broken only by a melodic warbling from somewhere above.
words.origin: Late Middle English: from Old Northern French werble, from werbler, of Germanic origin.

words.definition: The vault of the sky; heaven.
words.example: The craft pushed higher and higher, pushing through the welkin until it was surrounded by a mix of deep blue and countless stars.
words.origin: Old English wolcen 'cloud, sky', of Germanic origin.

words.definition: The melancholy feeling when you realize that life and the world will never be what you'd like it to be. Once described as the inherent sadness of mortality.
words.example: Brian's long bouts of weltschmerz made him think he was a romantic poet, but most people just thought he was depressed.
words.origin: Mid 19th century: from German Welt 'world' and Schmerz 'pain'.

words.definition: Go in a specified direction, typically slowly or by an indirect route.
words.example: They wended their way downtown, pausing to look at the department store windows and buy coffee from local cafes.
words.origin: Old English wendan 'to turn, depart', of Germanic origin; related to wind and wander.

words.definition: To turn (as a dish or vessel) upside down, usually to cover something.
words.example: He whelved a bowl over the receipt so that his wife wouldn't see how much he spent on groceries.
words.origin: Middle English: of obscure origin, probably related to dialectal whelm 'to turn over, cover'.

words.definition: In a left-handed, wrong or contrary direction; counterclockwise.
words.example: She spun three times widdershins on her left heel and then spoke the curse.
words.origin: Early 16th century: from Middle Low German weddersins, from Middle High German widersinnes, from wider 'against' and sin 'direction'.

words.definition: Tall, slender and graceful.
words.example: Willowy, well-dressed and smiling radiantly, Gisele caught everyone's attention as she entered the room.
words.origin: Late 18th century: from willow (the tree, noted for its slender flexible branches) plus -y.
