words.definition: Skilled at gaining an advantage, especially deceitfully.
words.example: Although he came off quite charming, the old politician was quite wily and didn't mind pushing a few friends under the bus to get ahead.
words.origin: Middle English: from wile 'a stratagem' plus -y, perhaps of Scandinavian origin.

words.definition: As a verb, to pry out or extract something. As in pulling the snail from a periwinkle.
words.example: Ross showed no inclination to leave his seat next to Beth, but Jeremy was determined to winkle him out of that chair one way or another.
words.origin: Late 19th century (as a verb): from the noun winkle, short for periwinkle, the edible sea snail one picks from its shell.

words.definition: Innocently charming, engaging, appealing.
words.example: "To the fancy there is nothing so winsome as a white sail seaward blown, unless it be a white sail homeward bound..." (From the novel "Ben Hur," by Lew Wallace)
words.origin: Old English wynsum, from wynn 'joy' plus -some.

words.definition: Sad or miserable in appearance.
words.example: He sat alone on the stoop, a woebegone expression on his face.
words.origin: Middle English: from woe plus begone, from Old English begān 'go round, surround'.

words.definition: Someone's usual way of doing things; accustomed.
words.example: He's wont to use a three-syllable word when a one-syllable word would suffice. ... OR ... As was her wont, she barged right in on the meeting.
words.origin: Old English gewunod, past participle of gewunian 'dwell, be accustomed'.

words.definition: Idle daydreaming.
words.example: I told my boss I was being creative while staring out the window -- but I was only woolgathering, my mind a perfect vacuum.
words.origin: Mid 16th century: originally denoting the gathering of fragments of wool caught on bushes, alluding to wandering aimlessly.

words.definition: Loud and sustained or repetitive noise.
words.example: He tried to focus on his report, but the yammer filling the open work space made that impossible.
words.origin: Late Middle English (in the sense 'lament'): probably from Middle Dutch jammeren, of Germanic origin.

words.definition: A harsh or hoarse cry or yelp.
words.example: The dog caught his tail in the sliding door, letting out a yawp of surprise.
words.origin: Middle English: imitative of a harsh cry, perhaps from Middle Dutch.

words.definition: Named or so-called.
words.example: Archie MacDonald's mysterious client, yclept Miss Braun, paid his retainer with gold Double Eagle coins.
words.origin: Old English geclipod, past participle of clipian 'to call, name'.

words.definition: Pleasingly plump; alluringly well-proportioned. ... Another happy contribution of Yiddish to English.
words.example: Super-models aren't my type; I prefer someone a bit more zaftig, like Renee Zellweger in "Bridget Jones."
words.origin: 1930s: from Yiddish zaftik 'juicy', from zaft 'juice, sap'.

words.definition: A light, gentle breeze.
words.example: The sun beat down upon my head; not even a zephyr was blowing to alleviate the discomfort.
words.origin: Old English zefferus, denoting a personification of the west wind, from Latin zephyrus, from Greek zephuros.

words.definition: Proceeding by inquiry.
words.example: The researcher's zetetic approach required patience but led to groundbreaking discoveries.
words.origin: Mid 17th century: from Greek zetetikos, from zetein 'to seek, inquire'.

words.definition: A drowsy and stupid state of mind.
words.example: I rushed out the door without my coffee and, as a result, sat through the meetings in a zwodder.
words.origin: English dialect (West Country): of obscure origin, perhaps related to Dutch zwadderen 'to splash, be sluggish'.

words.definition: Having a pleasant sound.
words.example: The tintinnabulation of the bells was particularly euphonious.
words.origin: Early 18th century: from Greek euphonos (from eu 'well' and phone 'sound') plus -ious.

words.definition: Ragged or disreputable in appearance.
words.example: For hippies, the tatterdemalion dress was as much a statement as it was a style.
words.origin: Early 17th century: from tatter, perhaps with a mock-French or mock-Italian ending of fanciful origin.
